The Game Boy hardware reference [Pan Docs](https://gbdev.io/pandocs/Graphics), particularly the section about graphics.

`rgbgfx` was originally written by stag019 as a program to be packaged in RGBDS. It was later rewritten by ISSOtm, and is now maintained by a number of contributors at [https://github.com/gbdev/rgbds](https://github.com/gbdev/rgbds).

`rgblink`

\[`[-d](#d)[h](#h)[M](#M)[t](#t)[V](#V)[v](#v)[w](#w)[x](#x)`\] \[`[-B](#B)` param\] \[`--color` when\] \[`[-l](#l)` linker\_script\] \[`[-m](#m)` map\_file\] \[`[-n](#n)` sym\_file\] \[`[-O](#O)` overlay\_file\] \[`[-o](#o)` out\_file\] \[`[-p](#p)` pad\_value\] \[`[-S](#S)` spec\] \[`[-W](#W)` warning\] file ...

The `rgblink` program links RGB object files, typically created by [rgbasm(1)](./rgbasm.1), into a single Game Boy ROM file. The object file format is documented in [rgbds(5)](./rgbds.5).

ROM0 sections are placed in the first 16 KiB of the output ROM, and ROMX sections are placed in any 16 KiB "bank" except the first. If your ROM will only be 32 KiB, you can use the `-t` option to change this.

Similarly, WRAM0 sections are placed in the first 4 KiB of WRAM ("bank 0"), and WRAMX sections are placed in any bank of the last 4 KiB. If your ROM doesn't use banked WRAM, you can use the `-w` option to change this.

Also, if your ROM is designed for a monochrome Game Boy, you can make sure that you don't use any incompatible section by using the `-d` option, which implies `-w` but also prohibits the use of banked VRAM.

`rgblink` accepts the usual short and long options, such as `-V` and `--version`. Options later in the command line override those set earlier, except for when duplicate options are considered an error. Options can be abbreviated as long as the abbreviation is unambiguous: `--verb` is `--verbose`, but `--ver` is invalid because it could also be `--version`.

Unless otherwise noted, passing '-' (a single dash) as a file name makes `rgblink` use standard input (for input files) or standard output (for output files). To suppress this behavior, and open a file in the current directory actually called '-', pass './-' instead. Using standard input or output for more than one file in a single command may produce unexpected results.

`rgblink` accepts decimal, hexadecimal, octal, and binary for numeric option arguments. Decimal numbers are written as usual; hexadecimal numbers must be prefixed with either '$' or '0x'; octal numbers must be prefixed with either '&' or '0o'; and binary numbers must be prefixed with either '%' or '0b'. (The prefixes '$' and '&' will likely need escaping or quoting to avoid being interpreted by the shell.) Leading zeros (after the base prefix, if any) are accepted, and letters are not case-sensitive. For example, all of these are equivalent: '42', '042', '0x2A', '0X2A', '0x2a', '&52', '0o52', '0O052', '0b00101010', '0B101010'.

The following options are accepted:

[`-B`](#B) param, `--backtrace` param

Configures how location backtraces are printed if warnings or errors occur. This flag may be specified multiple times with different parameters that combine meaningfully. If param is a positive number, it specifies the maximum backtrace depth, abbreviating deeper ones. Other valid parameter values are the following:

[`0`](#0)

Do not limit the maximum backtrace depth; this is the default.

[`all`](#all)

Force all locations to be printed, even "quiet" ones (see "Excluding locations from backtraces" in [rgbasm(5)](./rgbasm.5) for details).

[`no-all`](#no-all)

Do not print "quieted" locations in backtraces; this is the default.

[`collapse`](#collapse)

Print all locations on one line.

[`no-collapse`](#no-collapse)

Print one location per line; this is the default.

[`--color`](#color) when

Specify when to highlight warning and error messages with color: 'always', 'never', or 'auto'. 'auto' determines whether to use colors based on the '[NO_COLOR](https://no-color.org/)' or '[FORCE_COLOR](https://force-color.org/)' environment variables, or whether the output is to a TTY.

[`-d`](#d), `--dmg`

Enable DMG mode. Prohibit the use of sections that doesn't exist on a DMG, such as VRAM bank 1. This option automatically enables `-w`.

[`-h`](#h), `--help`

Print help text for the program and exit.

[`-l`](#l) linker\_script, `--linkerscript` linker\_script

Specify a linker script file that tells the linker how sections must be placed in the ROM. The attributes assigned in the linker script must be consistent with any assigned in the code. See [rgblink(5)](./rgblink.5) for more information about the linker script format.

[`-M`](#M), `--no-sym-in-map`

If specified, the map file will not list symbols, only sections.

[`-m`](#m) map\_file, `--map` map\_file

Write a map file to the given filename, listing how sections and symbols were assigned.

[`-n`](#n) sym\_file, `--sym` sym\_file

Write a symbol file to the given filename, listing all visible labels and exported numeric constants. Labels output their bank and address, numeric constants output their value, following [this specification](https://rgbds.gbdev.io/sym/). Several external programs can use this information, for example to help debugging ROMs.

[`-O`](#O) overlay\_file, `--overlay` overlay\_file

If specified, sections will be overlaid "on top" of the ROM image overlay\_file: empty space between sections will be filled by the corresponding bytes from overlay\_file. This is useful to patch an existing ROM. Note that all sections must be fixed (forced bank [**and**](#and) address)!

[`-o`](#o) out\_file, `--output` out\_file

Write the ROM image to the given file.

[`-p`](#p) pad\_value, `--pad` pad\_value

When inserting padding between sections, pad with this value. The default is 0.

[`-S`](#S) spec, `--scramble` spec

Enables a different "scrambling" algorithm for placing sections. See [Scrambling algorithm](#Scrambling_algorithm) below for an explanation and a description of spec.

[`-t`](#t), `--tiny`

Expand the ROM0 section size from 16 KiB to the full 32 KiB assigned to ROM. ROMX sections that are fixed to a bank other than 1 become errors, other ROMX sections are treated as ROM0. Useful for ROMs that fit in 32 KiB.

[`-V`](#V), `--version`

Print the version of the program and exit.

[`-v`](#v), `--verbose`

Be verbose. The verbosity level is increased by one each time the flag is specified, with each level including the previous:

1. Print the `rgblink` configuration before taking actions.
2. Print a notice before significant actions.
3. Print some of the actions' intermediate results.
4. Print some internal debug information.
5. Print detailed internal information.

The verbosity level does not go past 6.

Note that verbose output is only intended to be consumed by humans, and may change without notice between RGBDS releases; relying on those for scripts is not advised.

[`-W`](#W) warning, `--warning` warning

Set warning flag warning. A warning message will be printed if warning is an unknown warning flag. See the [DIAGNOSTICS](#DIAGNOSTICS) section for a list of warnings.

[`-w`](#w), `--wramx`

Expand the WRAM0 section size from 4 KiB to the full 8 KiB assigned to WRAM. WRAMX sections that are fixed to a bank other than 1 become errors, other WRAMX sections are treated as WRAM0.

[`-x`](#x), `--nopad`

Disables padding the end of the final file. This option automatically enables `-t`. You can use this to make binary files that are not a ROM. When making a ROM, note that not using this is not a replacement for [rgbfix(1)](./rgbfix.1)'s `-p` option!

@at\_file

Read more options and arguments from a file, as if its contents were given on the command line. Arguments are separated by whitespace or newlines. Lines starting with a hash sign ('#') are considered comments and ignored.

No shell processing is performed, such as wildcard or variable expansion. There is no support for escaping or quoting whitespace to be included in arguments. The standard '--' to stop option processing also disables at-file processing. Note that while '--' can be used [*inside*](#inside) an at-file, it only disables option processing within that at-file, and processing continues in the parent scope.

The default section placement algorithm tries to place sections into as few banks as possible. (It turns out that section placement is an NP-complete problem known as "bin packing", so `rgblink` does not attempt to find the optimal solution, but instead uses a "first-fit" heuristic to find a good one in a reasonable amount of time. There are no guarantees about where this algorithm will place sections, apart from the bank, address, and alignment constraints manually specified for the sections.)

"Scrambling" instead places sections into a given pool of banks, trying to minimize the number of sections sharing a given bank. This is useful to catch broken bank assumptions, such as expecting two different sections to land in the same bank (that is not guaranteed unless both are manually assigned the same bank number).

A scrambling spec is a comma-separated list of region specs. A trailing comma is allowed, as well as whitespace between all specs and their components. Each region spec has the following form:

region\[=size\]

region must be one of the following (case-insensitive), while size must be a positive decimal integer between 1 and the corresponding maximum. Certain regions allow omitting the size, in which case it defaults to its max value.

Region name

Max size

Size optional

[`romx`](#romx)

65535

No

[`sram`](#sram)

255

No

[`wramx`](#wramx)

7

Yes

A size of 0 disables scrambling for that region.

For example, 'romx=64,wramx=4' will scramble `ROMX` sections among ROM banks 1 to 64, `WRAMX` sections among RAM banks 1 to 4, and will not scramble `SRAM` sections.

Later region specs override earlier ones; for example, 'romx=42, Romx=0' disables scrambling for `romx`.

`wramx` scrambling is silently ignored if `-w` is passed (including if implied by `-d`), as `WRAMX` sections will be treated as `WRAM0`.

Warnings are diagnostic messages that indicate possibly erroneous behavior that does not necessarily compromise the linking process. The following options alter the way warnings are processed.

[`-Werror`](#Werror)

Make all warnings into errors. This can be negated as `-Wno-error` to prevent turning all warnings into errors.

[`-Werror=`](#Werror=)

Make the specified warning or meta warning into an error. A warning's name is appended (example: `-Werror=obsolete`), and this warning is implicitly enabled and turned into an error. This can be negated as `-Wno-error=` to prevent turning a specified warning into an error, even if `-Werror` is in effect.

The following warnings are "meta" warnings, that enable a collection of other warnings. If a specific warning is toggled via a meta flag and a specific one, the more specific one takes priority. The position on the command-line acts as a tie breaker, the last one taking effect.

[`-Wall`](#Wall)

This enables warnings that are likely to indicate an error or undesired behavior, and that can easily be fixed.

[`-Weverything`](#Weverything)

Enables literally every warning.

The following warnings are actual warning flags; with each description, the corresponding warning flag is included. Note that each of these flags also has a negation (for example, `-Wobsolete` enables the warning that `-Wno-obsolete` disables; and `-Wall` enables every warning that `-Wno-all` disables). Only the non-default flag is listed here. Ignoring the "no-" prefix, entries are listed alphabetically.

[`-Wno-assert`](#Wno-assert)

Warn when `WARN`-type assertions fail. (See "Aborting the assembly process" in [rgbasm(5)](./rgbasm.5) for `ASSERT`).

[`-Wdiv`](#Wdiv)

Warn when dividing the smallest negative integer (-2\*\*31) by -1, which yields itself due to integer overflow. This warning is enabled by `-Wall`.

[`-Wno-obsolete`](#Wno-obsolete)

Warn when obsolete features are encountered, which have been deprecated and may later be removed.

[`-Wshift`](#Wshift)

Warn when shifting right a negative value. Use a division by 2\*\*N instead. This warning is enabled by `-Wall`.

[`-Wshift-amount`](#Wshift-amount)

Warn when a shift's operand is negative or greater than 32. This warning is enabled by `-Wall`.

[`-Wtruncation=`](#Wtruncation=)

Warn when an implicit truncation (for example, `db` to an 8-bit value) loses some bits. `-Wtruncation=0` or `-Wno-truncation` disables this warning. `-Wtruncation=1` or just `-Wtruncation` warns when an N-bit value is 2\*\*N or greater, or less than -2\*\*N. `-Wtruncation=2` also warns when an N-bit value is less than -2\*\*(N-1), which will not fit in two's complement encoding.

All you need for a basic ROM is an object file, which can be made into a ROM image like so:

`$ rgblink -o bar.gb foo.o`

The resulting bar.gb will not have correct checksums (unless you put them in the assembly source). You should use [rgbfix(1)](./rgbfix.1) to fix these so that the program will actually run in a Game Boy:

`$ rgbfix -v bar.gb`

Here is a more complete example:

`$ rgblink -o bin/game.gb -n bin/game.sym -p 0xFF obj/title.o obj/engine.o`

[rgbasm(1)](./rgbasm.1), [rgblink(5)](./rgblink.5), [rgbfix(1)](./rgbfix.1), [rgbgfx(1)](./rgbgfx.1), [gbz80(7)](./gbz80.7), [rgbds(5)](./rgbds.5), [rgbds(7)](./rgbds.7)

`rgblink` was originally written by Carsten Sørensen as part of the ASMotor package, and was later repackaged in RGBDS by Justin Lloyd. It is now maintained by a number of contributors at [https://github.com/gbdev/rgbds](https://github.com/gbdev/rgbds).

The linker script is a file that allows specifying attributes for sections at link time, and in a centralized manner. There can only be one linker script per invocation of `rgblink`, but it can be split into several files (using the `INCLUDE` directive).

The linker script syntax is line-based. Each line may have a directive or section name, a comment, both, or neither. Whitespace (space and tab characters) is used to separate syntax elements, but is otherwise ignored.

Comments begin with a semicolon ‘`;`’ character, until the end of the line. They are simply ignored.

Keywords are composed of letters and digits (but they can't start with a digit); they are all case-insensitive.

Numbers can be written in a number of formats.

[**Format type**](#Format)

[**Possible prefixes**](#Possible)

[**Accepted characters**](#Accepted)

Decimal

none

0123456789

Hexadecimal

[`$`](#$), `0x`, `0X`

0123456789ABCDEF

Octal

[`&`](#&), `0o`, `0O`

01234567

Binary

[`%`](#_), `0b`, `0B`

01

Underscores are also accepted in numbers, except at the beginning of one. This can be useful for grouping digits, like ‘`1_234`’ or ‘`$ff_80`’.

Strings begin with a double quote, and end at the next (non-escaped) double quote. Strings must not contain literal newline characters. Most of the same character escapes as [rgbasm(5)](./rgbasm.5) are supported, specifically ‘`\\`’, ‘`\"`’, ‘`\n`’, ‘`\r`’, ‘`\t`’, and ‘`\0`’. Other backslash escape sequences in [rgbasm(5)](./rgbasm.5) are only relevant to assembly code and do not apply in linker scripts.

Including other files

‘`` `INCLUDE` path ``’ acts as if the contents of the file at path were copy-pasted in place of the `INCLUDE` directive. path must be a string.

Specifying the active bank

The active bank can be set by specifying its type (memory region) and number. The possible types are: `ROM0`, `ROMX`, `VRAM`, `SRAM`, `WRAM0`, `WRAMX`, `OAM`, and `HRAM`. The bank number can be omitted from the types that only contain a single bank, which are: `ROM0`, `ROMX` if `-t` is passed to [rgblink(1)](./rgblink.1), `VRAM` if `-d` is passed to [rgblink(1)](./rgblink.1), `WRAM0`, `WRAMX` if `-w` is passed to [rgblink(1)](./rgblink.1), `OAM`, and `HRAM`. (`SRAM` is the only type that can never have its bank number omitted.)

After a bank specification, the "current address" is set to the last value it had for that bank. If the bank has never been active thus far, the "current address" defaults to the beginning of the bank (e.g. $4000 for `ROMX` sections).

Instead of giving a bank number, the keyword `FLOATING` can be used instead; this sets the type of the subsequent sections without binding them to a particular bank. (If the type only allows a single bank, e.g. `ROM0`, then `FLOATING` is valid but redundant and has no effect.) Since no particular section is active, the "current address" is made floating (as if by a ‘`` `FLOATING` ``’ directive), and `ORG` is not allowed.

Changing the current address

A bank must be active for any of these directives to be used.

‘`` `ORG` addr ``’ sets the "current address" to addr. This directive cannot be used to move the address backwards: addr must be greater than or equal to the "current address".

‘`` `FLOATING` ``’ causes all sections between it and the next `ORG` or bank specification to be placed at addresses automatically determined by `rgblink`. (It is, however, compatible with `ALIGN` below.)

‘`` `ALIGN` addr, offset ``’ increases the "current address" until it is aligned to the specified boundary (i.e. the align lowest bits of the address are equal to offset). If offset is omitted, it is implied to be 0. For example, if the "current address" is $0007, ‘`ALIGN 8`’ would set it to $0100, and ‘`ALIGN 8, 10`’ would set it to $000A.

‘`` `DS` size ``’ increases the "current address" by size. The gap is not allocated, so smaller floating sections can later be placed there.

A section can be placed simply by naming it (with a string). Its bank is set to the active bank, and its address to the "current address". Any constraints the section already possesses (whether from earlier in the linker script, or from the object files being linked) must be consistent with what the linker script specifies: the section's type must match, the section's bank number (if set) must match the active bank, etc. In particular, if the section has an alignment constraint, the address at which it is placed by the linker script must obey that constraint; otherwise, an error will occur.

After a section is placed, the "current address" is increased by the section's size. This must not increase it past the end of the active memory region.

The section must have been defined in the object files being linked, unless the section name is followed by the keyword `OPTIONAL`.

; This line contains only a comment
ROMX $F            ; start a bank
  "Some functions" ; a section name
  ALIGN 8          ; a directive
  "Some \\"array\\""

WRAMX 2            ; start another bank
  org $d123        ; another directive
  "Some variables"
[rgbasm(1)](./rgbasm.1), [rgbasm(5)](./rgbasm.5), [rgblink(1)](./rgblink.1), [rgbfix(1)](./rgbfix.1), [rgbgfx(1)](./rgbgfx.1), [gbz80(7)](./gbz80.7), [rgbds(5)](./rgbds.5), [rgbds(7)](./rgbds.7)

[rgblink(1)](./rgblink.1) was originally written by Carsten Sørensen as part of the ASMotor package, and was later repackaged in RGBDS by Justin Lloyd. It is now maintained by a number of contributors at [https://github.com/gbdev/rgbds](https://github.com/gbdev/rgbds).

# LCD Control

## FF40 — LCDC: LCD control

**LCDC** is the main **LCD C**ontrol register at address $FF40. Its bits toggle what elements are displayed on the screen, and how.

Bit layout:
- Bit 7: LCD & PPU enable (0 = Off; 1 = On)
- Bit 6: Window tile map area (0 = $9800–$9BFF; 1 = $9C00–$9FFF)
- Bit 5: Window enable (0 = Off; 1 = On)
- Bit 4: BG & Window tile data area (0 = $8800–$97FF; 1 = $8000–$8FFF)
- Bit 3: BG tile map area (0 = $9800–$9BFF; 1 = $9C00–$9FFF)
- Bit 2: OBJ size (0 = 8×8; 1 = 8×16)
- Bit 1: OBJ enable (0 = Off; 1 = On)
- Bit 0: BG & Window enable / priority (Different meaning in CGB Mode)

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control

text:
### LCDC.7 — LCD enable

This bit controls whether the LCD is on and the PPU is active. Setting it to 0 turns both off, which grants immediate and full access to VRAM, OAM, etc.

**CAUTION**: Stopping LCD operation (Bit 7 from 1 to 0) may be performed during VBlank ONLY. Disabling the display outside of the VBlank period may damage the hardware by burning in a black horizontal line similar to that which appears when the GB is turned off. This appears to be a serious issue. Nintendo is reported to reject any games not following this rule.

When the display is disabled the screen is blank, which on DMG is displayed as a white whiter than color #0.

On SGB, the screen doesn't turn white; it appears that the previous picture sticks to the screen.

When re-enabling the LCD, the PPU will immediately start drawing again, but the screen will stay blank during the first frame.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.7 — LCD enable

text:
### LCDC.6 — Window tile map area

This bit controls which background map the Window uses for rendering. When it's clear (0), the $9800 tilemap is used, otherwise it's the $9C00 one.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.6 — Window tile map area

text:
### LCDC.5 — Window enable

This bit controls whether the window shall be displayed or not.

This bit is overridden on DMG by [bit 0](#LCDC.0) if that bit is clear.

Changing the value of this register mid-frame triggers several more complex behaviours; see the Window mid-frame behavior chapter for details.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.5 — Window enable

text:
### LCDC.4 — BG and Window tile data area

This bit controls which addressing mode the BG and Window use to pick tiles.

- Bit 4 = 0: Tile data area is $8800–$97FF (signed tile IDs)
- Bit 4 = 1: Tile data area is $8000–$8FFF (unsigned tile IDs)

Objects (sprites) aren't affected by this, and will always use the $8000 addressing mode.

metadata.section: LCDC.4 — BG and Window tile data area
metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.4 — BG and Window tile data area

text:
### LCDC.3 — BG tile map area

This bit works similarly to LCDC bit 6: if the bit is clear (0), the BG uses tilemap $9800, otherwise tilemap $9C00.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.3 — BG tile map area

text:
### LCDC.2 — OBJ size

This bit controls the size of all objects (1 tile or 2 stacked vertically).

- Bit 2 = 0: Objects are 8×8 pixels
- Bit 2 = 1: Objects are 8×16 pixels

Be cautious when changing object size mid-frame. Changing from 8×8 to 8×16 pixels mid-frame within 8 scanlines of the bottom of an object causes the object's second tile to be visible for the rest of those 8 lines.

If the size is changed during mode 2 or 3, remnants of objects in range could leak into the other tile and cause artifacts.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.2 — OBJ size

text:
### LCDC.1 — OBJ enable

This bit toggles whether objects are displayed or not.

This can be toggled mid-frame, for example to avoid objects being displayed on top of a status bar or text box.

Note: Toggling mid-scanline might have funky results on DMG; investigation needed.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.1 — OBJ enable

text:
### LCDC.0 — BG and Window enable/priority

LCDC.0 has different meanings depending on Game Boy type and Mode:

**Non-CGB Mode (DMG, SGB and CGB in compatibility mode): BG and Window display**
When Bit 0 is cleared, both background and window become blank (white), and the Window Display Bit (bit 5) is ignored in that case. Only objects may still be displayed (if enabled in Bit 1).

**CGB Mode: BG and Window master priority**
When Bit 0 is cleared, the background and window lose their priority — the objects will always be displayed on top of background and window, independently of the priority flags in OAM and BG Map attributes.

When Bit 0 is set, pixel priority is resolved as described in the BG-to-OBJ Priority in CGB Mode section.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Control > FF40 — LCDC: LCD control > LCDC.0 — BG and Window enable/priority

text:
## Using LCDC

LCDC is a powerful tool: each bit controls a lot of behavior, and can be modified at any time during the frame.

One of the important aspects of LCDC is that unlike VRAM, the PPU never locks it. It's thus possible to modify it mid-scanline!

## Faux-layer textbox/status bar

A problem often seen in 8-bit games is objects rendering on top of the textbox/status bar. It's possible to prevent this using LCDC if the textbox/status bar is alone on its scanlines:

- Set LCDC.1 to 1 for gameplay scanlines
- Set LCDC.1 to 0 for textbox/status bar scanlines

Usually, these bars are either at the top or bottom of the screen, so the bit can be set by the VBlank and/or STAT handlers.

Hiding objects behind a right-side window is more challenging.

# LCD Status Registers

**Terminology**: A *dot* is the shortest period over which the PPU can output one pixel. It is equivalent to 1 T-cycle on DMG or on CGB Normal Speed mode, or 2 T-cycles on CGB Double Speed mode. On each dot during mode 3, either the PPU outputs a pixel or the fetcher is stalling the FIFOs.

## FF44 — LY: LCD Y coordinate [read-only]

LY indicates the current horizontal line, which might be about to be drawn, being drawn, or just been drawn. LY can hold any value from 0 to 153, with values from 144 to 153 indicating the VBlank period.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Status Registers > FF44 — LY: LCD Y coordinate [read-only]

text:
## FF45 — LYC: LY compare

The Game Boy constantly compares the value of the LYC and LY registers. When both values are identical, the LYC=LY flag in the STAT register is set, and (if enabled) a STAT interrupt is requested.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Status Registers > FF45 — LYC: LY compare

text:
## FF41 — STAT: LCD status

Bit layout:
- Bit 7: (unused)
- Bit 6: LYC int select (Read/Write) — If set, selects the LYC == LY condition for the STAT interrupt.
- Bit 5: Mode 2 int select (Read/Write) — If set, selects the Mode 2 condition for the STAT interrupt.
- Bit 4: Mode 1 int select (Read/Write) — If set, selects the Mode 1 condition for the STAT interrupt.
- Bit 3: Mode 0 int select (Read/Write) — If set, selects the Mode 0 condition for the STAT interrupt.
- Bit 2: LYC == LY (Read-only) — Set when LY contains the same value as LYC; it is constantly updated.
- Bits 1-0: PPU mode (Read-only) — Indicates the PPU's current status. Reports 0 instead when the PPU is disabled.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Status Registers > FF41 — STAT: LCD status

text:
### Spurious STAT interrupts

A hardware quirk in the monochrome Game Boy makes the LCD interrupt sometimes trigger when writing to STAT (including writing $00) during OAM scan, HBlank, VBlank, or LY=LYC. It behaves as if $FF were written for one M-cycle, and then the written value were written the next M-cycle.

Because the GBC in DMG mode does not have this quirk, two games that depend on this quirk (Ocean's Road Rash and Vic Tokai's Xerd no Densetsu) will not run on a GBC.

metadata.heading_path: LCD Status Registers > FF41 — STAT: LCD status > Spurious STAT interrupts

text:
## FF47 — BGP (Non-CGB Mode only): BG palette data

This register assigns gray shades to the color indices of the BG and Window tiles.

Bit layout:
- Bits 7-6: Color for ID 3
- Bits 5-4: Color for ID 2
- Bits 3-2: Color for ID 1
- Bits 1-0: Color for ID 0

Each two-bit value maps to a color:
- Value 0: White
- Value 1: Light gray
- Value 2: Dark gray
- Value 3: Black

In CGB Mode the color palettes are taken from CGB palette memory (CRAM) instead.

metadata.section: FF47 — BGP (Non-CGB Mode only): BG palette data
metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Monochrome Palettes > FF47 — BGP

text:
## FF48–FF49 — OBP0, OBP1 (Non-CGB Mode only): OBJ palette 0, 1 data

These registers assign gray shades to the color indexes of the OBJs that use the corresponding palette.
They work exactly like BGP ($FF47), except that the lower two bits are ignored because color index 0 is transparent for OBJs.

metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Monochrome Palettes > FF48–FF49 — OBP0, OBP1

text:
## LCD Color Palettes (CGB only)

The CGB has a small amount of RAM used to store its color palettes. Palette RAM (or CRAM for Color RAM) is not accessed directly, but instead through registers.

### FF68 — BCPS/BGPI (CGB Mode only): Background color palette specification / Background palette index

This register addresses a byte in the CGB's background palette RAM. There are 8 palettes × 4 colors/palette × 2 bytes/color = 64 bytes that can be addressed.

Order: BGP0 color 0, BGP0 color 1, BGP0 color 2, BGP0 color 3, BGP1 color 0, and so on. Address $03 accesses the second (upper) byte of BGP0 color #1 via BCPD.

Bit layout:
- Bit 7: Auto-increment (0 = Disabled; 1 = Increment Address field after writing to BCPD/OCPD, even during Mode 3 despite the write failing; reads never cause increment)
- Bits 5-0: Address — Specifies which byte of BG Palette Memory can be accessed

Unlike BCPD, this register can be accessed outside VBlank and HBlank.

### FF69 — BCPD/BGPD (CGB Mode only): Background color palette data / Background palette data

This register reads/writes data to the CGB background palette memory, addressed through BCPS. Each color is stored as little-endian RGB555:
- Bits 0-4: Red intensity
- Bits 5-9: Green intensity
- Bits 10-14: Blue intensity

Much like VRAM, data in palette memory cannot be read or written during Mode 3 (when the PPU is reading from it).

All background colors are initialized as white by the boot ROM, but it is a good idea to initialize all colors yourself (e.g., for soft-reset).

metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Color Palettes (CGB only) > BCPS/BCPD

text:
### FF6A–FF6B — OCPS/OBPI, OCPD/OBPD (CGB Mode only): OBJ color palette specification / OBJ palette index, OBJ color palette data / OBJ palette data

These registers function exactly like BCPS and BCPD respectively; the 64 bytes of OBJ palette memory are entirely separate from Background palette memory, but function the same.

Note that while 4 colors are stored per OBJ palette, color #0 is never used, as it's always transparent. It's fine to write garbage values or leave color #0 uninitialized.

In CGB mode, the boot ROM leaves all object colors uninitialized (and thus somewhat random/unreliable), aside from setting the first byte of OBJ0 color #0 to $00, which is unused.

In DMG compatibility mode, the boot ROM sets the first 2 object palettes which are used by OBP0/OBP1.

metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Color Palettes (CGB only) > OCPS/OCPD

text:
### RGB Translation by CGBs

When developing graphics on PCs, note that the RGB values will have different appearance on CGB displays as on VGA/HDMI monitors calibrated to sRGB color. Because the GBC is not lit, the highest intensity will produce light gray rather than white. The intensities are not linear; the values $10-$1F will all appear very bright, while medium and darker colors are ranged at $00-0F.

The CGB display's pigments aren't perfectly saturated. Increasing the intensity of only one R/G/B color will also influence the other two R/G/B colors. For example, a color setting of $03EF (Blue=$00, Green=$1F, Red=$0F) will appear as Neon Green on VGA displays, but on the CGB it'll produce a decently washed out Yellow.

metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Color Palettes (CGB only) > RGB Translation by CGBs

text:
### RGB Translation by GBAs

Even though GBA is described to be compatible with CGB games, most CGB games are completely unplayable on older GBAs because most colors are invisible (black). Colors such as Black and White appear the same on both CGB and GBA, but medium intensities are arranged completely different. Intensities in range $00–07 are invisible/black on older GBAs.

**Workaround**: Newer CGB games may avoid this effect by changing palette data when detecting GBA hardware. Based on measurements using the 144p Test Suite, a fairly close approximation is: GBA = GBC × 3/4 + $08 for each R/G/B component.

This problem with low brightness levels does not affect later GBA SP units and Game Boy Player.

metadata.heading_path: Palettes > LCD Color Palettes (CGB only) > RGB Translation by GBAs

text:
# Memory Map

The Game Boy has a 16-bit address bus, which is used to address ROM, RAM, and I/O.

| Start | End | Description | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0000 | $3FFF | 16 KiB ROM bank 00 | From cartridge, usually a fixed bank |
| $4000 | $7FFF | 16 KiB ROM Bank 01–NN | From cartridge, switchable bank via mapper (if any) |
| $8000 | $9FFF | 8 KiB Video RAM (VRAM) | In CGB mode, switchable bank 0/1 |
| $A000 | $BFFF | 8 KiB External RAM | From cartridge, switchable bank if any |
| $C000 | $CFFF | 4 KiB Work RAM (WRAM) | |
| $D000 | $DFFF | 4 KiB Work RAM (WRAM) | In CGB mode, switchable bank 1–7 |
| $E000 | $FDFF | Echo RAM (mirror of $C000–$DDFF) | Nintendo says use of this area is prohibited. |
| $FE00 | $FE9F | Object attribute memory (OAM) | |
| $FEA0 | $FEFF | Not Usable | Nintendo says use of this area is prohibited. |
| $FF00 | $FF7F | I/O Registers | |
| $FF80 | $FFEE | High RAM (HRAM) | |
| $FFFF | $FFFF | Interrupt Enable register (IE) | |

## I/O Ranges

The Game Boy uses the following I/O ranges:

| Start | End | First appeared | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| $FF00 | — | DMG | Joypad input (P1/JOYP) |
| $FF01–$FF02 | — | DMG | Serial transfer (Link Cable) |
| $FF04–$FF07 | — | DMG | Timer and divider (DIV, TIMA, TMA, TAC) |
| $FF0F | — | DMG | Interrupts (IF: Interrupt flag) |
| $FF10–$FF26 | — | DMG | Audio registers |
| $FF30–$FF3F | — | DMG | Wave pattern RAM |
| $FF40–$FF4B | — | DMG | LCD Control, Status, Position, Scrolling, and Palettes |
| $FF46 | — | DMG | OAM DMA transfer |
| $FF4C–$FF4D | — | CGB | KEY0 (CPU mode select) and KEY1 (Prepare speed switch) |
| $FF4F | — | CGB | VRAM Bank Select (VBK) |
| $FF50 | — | DMG | Boot ROM mapping control |
| $FF51–$FF55 | — | CGB | VRAM DMA |
| $FF56 | — | CGB | IR port (RP: Infrared communications port) |
| $FF68–$FF6B | — | CGB | BG / OBJ Palettes (CRAM) |
| $FF6C | — | CGB | Object priority mode (OPRI) |
| $FF70 | — | CGB | WRAM Bank Select (SVBK/WBK) |

## VRAM memory map

VRAM is normal RAM, but the PPU interprets it in specific ways.

Bank 1 does not exist except on CGB, where it can be switched to (only in CGB Mode) using the VBK register ($FF4F).

Each bank first contains 384 tiles, of 16 bytes each. These tiles are commonly thought of as grouped in three blocks of 128 tiles each.

The ID of a tile can be obtained from its address: ID = address / 16 mod 256. This is equivalent to only looking at the address' middle two hexadecimal digits.

After the tiles, each bank contains two maps, 32×32 (= 1024) bytes each. Bank 0 contains tile maps, while bank 1 contains the corresponding attribute maps (CGB Mode only).

Each map entry corresponds to coordinates linked to its address:
- X = address mod 32
- Y = address / 32 mod 32

The address of any entry can be thought of as a bitfield: bits 15-12 are $101, bit 11 selects tilemap (0 or 1), bits 9-5 are Y coordinate, bits 4-0 are X coordinate.

## Jump Vectors in first ROM bank

The following addresses are supposed to be used as jump vectors:

- RST instructions: $0000, $0008, $0010, $0018, $0020, $0028, $0030, $0038
- Interrupts: $0040, $0048, $0050, $0058, $0060

However, this memory area ($0000-$00FF) may be used for any other purpose if your program doesn't use any (or only some) rst instructions or interrupts. rst is a 1-byte instruction that works similarly to the 3-byte call instruction, except the destination address is restricted. Since it is 1-byte sized, it is also slightly faster.

metadata.section: Jump Vectors in first ROM bank
metadata.heading_path: Memory Map > Jump Vectors in first ROM bank

text:
## Cartridge Header in first ROM bank

The memory area at $0100-$014F contains the cartridge header. This area contains information about the program, its entry point, checksums, information about the used MBC chip, the ROM and RAM sizes, etc. Most of the bytes in this area are required to be specified correctly.

metadata.section: Cartridge Header in first ROM bank
metadata.heading_path: Memory Map > Cartridge Header in first ROM bank

text:
## External Memory and Hardware

The areas from $0000-$7FFF and $A000-$BFFF address external hardware on the cartridge, which is essentially an expansion board. Typically this is a ROM and SRAM or, more often, a Memory Bank Controller (MBC). The RAM area can be read from and written to normally; writes to the ROM area control the MBC.

Some MBCs allow mapping of other hardware into the RAM area in this way.

Cartridge RAM is often battery buffered to hold saved game positions, high score tables, and other information when the Game Boy is turned off.

metadata.heading_path: Memory Map > External Memory and Hardware

text:
## Echo RAM

The range $E000-$FDFF is mapped to WRAM, but only the lower 13 bits of the address lines are connected, with the upper bits on the upper bank set internally in the memory controller by a bank swap register. This causes the address to effectively wrap around. All reads and writes to this range have the same effect as reads and writes to $C000-$DDFF.

Nintendo prohibits developers from using this memory range. The behavior is confirmed on all official hardware. Some emulators (such as VisualBoyAdvance <1.8) don't emulate Echo RAM.

In some flash cartridges, echo RAM interferes with SRAM normally at $A000-$BFFF. Software can check if Echo RAM is properly emulated by writing to RAM (avoid values $00 and $FF) and checking if said value is mirrored in Echo RAM and not cart SRAM.

## FEA0–FEFF range

Nintendo indicates use of this area is prohibited. This area returns $FF when OAM is blocked, and otherwise the behavior depends on the hardware revision.

- On DMG, MGB, SGB, and SGB2: reads during OAM block trigger OAM corruption. Reads otherwise return $00.
- On CGB revisions 0-D: this area is a unique RAM area, but is masked with a revision-specific value.
- On CGB revision E, AGB, AGS, and GBP: it returns the high nibble of the lower address byte twice (e.g., FFAx returns $AA, FFBx returns $BB).

## IME: Interrupt master enable flag [write only]

IME is a flag internal to the CPU that controls whether any interrupt handlers are called, regardless of the contents of IE.
IME cannot be read in any way, and is modified by these instructions/events only:

- ei: Enables interrupt handling (IME := 1)
- di: Disables interrupt handling (IME := 0)
- reti: Enables interrupts and returns (same as ei immediately followed by ret)
- When an interrupt handler is executed: Disables interrupts before calling the interrupt handler

IME is unset (interrupts disabled) when the game starts running.

The effect of ei is delayed by one instruction. This means that ei followed immediately by di does not allow any interrupts between them. This interacts with the halt bug in an interesting way.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupts > IME: Interrupt master enable flag [write only]

text:
## FFFF — IE: Interrupt enable

Bit layout:
- Bit 0: VBlank (Read/Write) — Controls whether the VBlank interrupt handler ($0040) may be called.
- Bit 1: LCD (Read/Write) — Controls whether the LCD/STAT interrupt handler ($0048) may be called.
- Bit 2: Timer (Read/Write) — Controls whether the Timer interrupt handler ($0050) may be called.
- Bit 3: Serial (Read/Write) — Controls whether the Serial interrupt handler ($0058) may be called.
- Bit 4: Joypad (Read/Write) — Controls whether the Joypad interrupt handler ($0060) may be called.

## FF0F — IF: Interrupt flag

Bit layout (same as IE):
- Bit 0: VBlank — Controls whether the VBlank interrupt is being requested.
- Bit 1: LCD — Controls whether the LCD/STAT interrupt is being requested.
- Bit 2: Timer — Controls whether the Timer interrupt is being requested.
- Bit 3: Serial — Controls whether the Serial interrupt is being requested.
- Bit 4: Joypad — Controls whether the Joypad interrupt is being requested.

When an interrupt request signal changes from low to high, the corresponding bit in IF becomes set (e.g., bit 0 when PPU enters VBlank).

Any set bits in IF are only requesting an interrupt. The actual execution happens only if both IME and the corresponding IE bit are set; otherwise the interrupt waits until both allow it.

The CPU automatically sets and clears IF bits, but the user may write to IF to manually request or discard interrupts.

metadata.heading_path: Interrupts > FFFF — IE: Interrupt enable / FF0F — IF: Interrupt flag

text:
## Interrupt handling

When an interrupt is serviced:

1. The IF bit corresponding to this interrupt and the IME flag are reset by the CPU. The former acknowledges the interrupt, while the latter prevents further interrupts until re-enabled (typically via reti).
2. The corresponding interrupt handler is called — a regular call like `call <address>` (current PC pushed onto stack, then set to handler address).

Interrupt service routine timing:
1. Two wait states are executed (2 M-cycles; presumably CPU executes nops).
2. Current PC value is pushed onto the stack (2 more M-cycles).
3. PC is set to the handler address ($40, $48, $50, $58, or $60) (1 M-cycle).

The entire process lasts 5 M-cycles.

## Interrupt priorities

When more than one bit in IF is set (multiple interrupts requested at once), the interrupt with the highest priority is serviced first. This can happen when:
1. More than one interrupt request signal changed from low to high simultaneously.
2. Several interrupts were requested while IME/IE didn't allow servicing.
3. The user wrote a value with several bits set to IF.

Priorities follow the order of bits in IE and IF: Bit 0 (VBlank) has highest priority, Bit 4 (Joypad) has lowest priority.

## Nested interrupt handling

The CPU automatically disables all other interrupts by setting IME=0 when it services an interrupt. Usually IME remains zero until the handler returns (setting IME=1 via reti).

However, if you want to allow servicing of other interrupts (of any priority) during execution of an interrupt handler, you may do so by using the ei instruction in the handler.

## INT $40 — VBlank interrupt

This interrupt is requested every time the Game Boy enters VBlank (Mode 1).

The VBlank interrupt occurs approximately 59.7 times a second on handheld Game Boy (DMG or CGB) or Game Boy Player, and approximately 61.1 times a second on Super Game Boy (SGB). This interrupt occurs at the beginning of the VBlank period (LY=144).

During this period video hardware is not using VRAM so it may be freely accessed. This period lasts approximately 1.1 milliseconds.

## INT $48 — STAT interrupt

There are various sources which can trigger this interrupt as described in the STAT register ($FF41).

The various STAT interrupt sources (modes 0-2 and LYC=LY) have their state (inactive=low, active=high) logically ORed into a shared STAT interrupt line if their respective enable bit is turned on.

A STAT interrupt is triggered by a rising edge (transition from low to high) on the STAT interrupt line.

**STAT blocking**: If a STAT interrupt source logically ORs the interrupt line high while it's already set high by another source, then there will be no low-to-high transition and so no interrupt will occur. For example, if interrupts are enabled for two consecutive modes such as Mode 0 and Mode 1, then no interrupt triggers for Mode 1 (since the STAT line won't go low between them).

**Using the STAT interrupt**: A popular use is to indicate when the PPU is about to redraw a given LCD line. This can dynamically control SCX/SCY ($FF43/$FF42) for special video effects. Example: set LYC to WY, enable LY=LYC interrupt, and have the handler disable objects — useful for hiding sprites behind text boxes.

## INT $50 — Timer interrupt

The timer interrupt is requested every time that the timer overflows (that is, when TIMA exceeds $FF).

## INT $58 — Serial interrupt

The serial interrupt is requested upon completion of a serial data transfer. In other words, eight serial clock cycles after starting a transfer (by setting SC bit 7), the incoming data will be in SB and the interrupt will be requested.

## INT $60 — Joypad interrupt

The Joypad interrupt is requested when any of P1 bits 0-3 change from High to Low. This happens when a button is pressed (provided action/direction buttons are enabled by bit 5/4 respectively). Due to switch bounce, one or more High-to-Low transitions are usually produced per press.

**Using the joypad interrupt**: Useful for identifying button presses if only action (bit 5) or direction (bit 4) is selected, but not both. If both are selected and a bit is already held Low by an action button, pressing the corresponding direction button makes no difference. The Joypad interrupt's main purpose is to terminate the STOP (low power) standby state. GBA SP seems unaffected by switch bounce.

# Object Attribute Memory (OAM)

The Game Boy PPU can display up to 40 movable objects (sprites), each 8×8 or 8×16 pixels. Only ten objects can be displayed per scanline.

Object tiles have the same format as BG tiles, but are taken from tile blocks 0 and 1 at $8000-$8FFF with unsigned numbering.

Object attributes reside in OAM at $FE00-$FE9F. Each of the 40 entries consists of four bytes.

## Byte 0 — Y Position

Y = Object's vertical position on the screen + 16.

Examples:
- Y=0: hides an object (on OAM area)
- Y=2: hides an 8×8 object but displays last two rows of an 8×16 object
- Y=16: displays at top of screen
- Y=144: displays an 8×16 object aligned with bottom of screen
- Y=152: displays an 8×8 object aligned with bottom of screen
- Y=154: displays first six rows of an object at bottom
- Y=160: hides an object (on LCD)

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Byte 0 — Y Position

text:
## Byte 1 — X Position

X = Object's horizontal position on the screen + 8.

An off-screen value (X=0 or X>=168) hides the object, but the object still contributes to the limit of ten objects per scanline. This can cause objects later in OAM not to be drawn on that line.
A better way to hide an object is to set its Y-coordinate off-screen.

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Byte 1 — X Position

text:
## Byte 2 — Tile Index

In 8×8 mode (LCDC bit 2 = 0): This byte specifies the object's tile index ($00-$FF). Unsigned value selecting a tile from $8000-$8FFF. In CGB Mode, could be in VRAM bank 0 or 1 depending on bit 3 of the next byte.

In 8×16 mode (LCDC bit 2 = 1): The memory area at $8000-$8FFF is still interpreted as 8×8 tiles where every 2 tiles form an object. This byte specifies the index of the first (top) tile. Hardware enforces: LSB of tile index is ignored; top tile is NN & $FE, bottom tile is NN | $01.

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Byte 2 — Tile Index

text:
## Byte 3 — Attributes/Flags

Bit layout:
- Bit 7: Priority (0 = OBJ over BG; 1 = BG color indices 1-3 drawn over this OBJ)
- Bit 6: Y flip (0 = Normal; 1 = Vertically mirrored)
- Bit 5: X flip (0 = Normal; 1 = Horizontally mirrored)
- Bit 4: DMG palette [Non CGB Mode only] (0 = OBP0; 1 = OBP1)
- Bit 3: Bank [CGB Mode Only] (0 = VRAM bank 0; 1 = VRAM bank 1)
- Bits 2-0: CGB palette [CGB Mode Only] — Which of OBP0-7 to use

## Writing data to OAM

The recommended method is to write data to a buffer in normal RAM (typically WRAM) first, then copy that buffer to OAM using the DMA transfer functionality.

Direct writes to OAM are possible by accessing it normally during HBlank and VBlank periods only.

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Writing data to OAM

text:
## Object Priority and Conflicts

Two kinds of priorities exist for objects: selection priority (which objects are ignored when >10 on a scanline) and drawing priority (which overlapping object is displayed on top).

### Selection priority

During each scanline's OAM scan, the PPU compares LY to each object's Y position (using LCDC bit 2 for size) to select up to 10 objects. The PPU scans OAM sequentially from $FE00 to $FE9F, selecting the first (up to) 10 suitably-positioned objects.

Since only Y coordinate is checked, off-screen objects count toward the 10-object limit. Setting X=0 or X>=168 hides an object but it still counts. To keep off-screen objects from affecting on-screen ones, set their Y to Y=0 or Y>=160 (or Y<=8 if OBJ size is 8×8).

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Object Priority and Conflicts > Selection priority

text:
### Drawing priority

When opaque pixels from two different objects overlap, the higher-priority object's pixel wins. Priority is determined differently by mode:

- **Non-CGB mode**: Smaller X coordinate = higher priority. When X coordinates are identical, the object first in OAM has higher priority.
- **CGB mode**: Only OAM location determines priority. Earlier object = higher priority.

**Interaction with BG over OBJ flag**: The PPU first resolves priority between objects to pick an object pixel (first non-transparent pixel iterating by drawing priority). The BG over OBJ attribute is never considered in this process. Only after object priority is resolved, the object pixel's BG over OBJ attribute determines whether it draws over background. A higher-priority object with BG over OBJ enabled will mask lower-priority objects even if those have BG over OBJ disabled.

In CGB Mode, BG vs OBJ priority is declared in more than one register; see BG-to-OBJ Priority in CGB Mode.

metadata.heading_path: Object Attribute Memory (OAM) > Object Priority and Conflicts > Drawing priority

text:
## FF04 — DIV: Divider register

This register is incremented at a rate of 16384 Hz (~16779 Hz on SGB). Writing any value to this register resets it to $00.

Additionally, DIV is reset when executing the stop instruction, and only begins ticking again once stop mode ends. This also occurs during a speed switch.

The divider is affected by CGB double speed mode, and will increment at 32768 Hz in double speed.

metadata.heading_path: Timer and Divider Registers > FF04 — DIV: Divider register

text:
## FF05 — TIMA: Timer counter

This timer is incremented at the clock frequency specified by TAC ($FF07). When it overflows (exceeds $FF), it resets to the value in TMA ($FF06) and a Timer interrupt ($0050) is requested.

## FF06 — TMA: Timer modulo

When TIMA overflows, it is reset to this register's value and an interrupt is requested.

Example: If TMA = $FF, an interrupt fires at the clock frequency selected in TAC (every increment is overflow). If TMA = $FE, interrupts fire every two increments (effectively dividing clock by 2). TMA = $FD divides by 3, and so on.

If a TMA write occurs on the same M-cycle as TIMA overflow transfers TMA to TIMA, the old value is transferred.

metadata.heading_path: Timer and Divider Registers > FF05 — TIMA / FF06 — TMA

text:
## FF07 — TAC: Timer control

Bit layout:
- Bit 2: Enable (controls whether TIMA is incremented; DIV always counts regardless)
- Bits 1-0: Clock select

Clock select frequencies:
| Select | Increment every | DMG/SGB2/CGB normal Hz | SGB1 Hz | CGB double speed Hz |
|--------|-----------------|----------------------|---------|--------------------|
| 00 | 256 M-cycles | 4096 | ~4194 | 8192 |
| 01 | 4 M-cycles | 262144 | ~268400 | 524288 |
| 10 | 16 M-cycles | 65536 | ~67110 | 131072 |
| 11 | 64 M-cycles | 16384 | ~16780 | 32768 |

Note: Writing to TAC may increase TIMA once (see Relation between Timer and Divider register).

metadata.heading_path: Timer and Divider Registers > FF07 — TAC: Timer control

text:
## FF46 — DMA: OAM DMA source address & start

Writing to this register starts a DMA transfer from ROM or RAM to OAM ($FE00-$FE9F). The written value specifies the source address divided by $100:

Source: $XX00-$XX9F (XX = $00 to $DF)
Destination: $FE00-$FE9F

The transfer takes 160 M-cycles: 640 dots (1.4 lines) in normal speed, or 320 dots (0.7 lines) in CGB Double Speed Mode. This is much faster than a CPU-driven copy.

metadata.section: FF46 — DMA: OAM DMA source address & start

text:
## OAM DMA bus conflicts

On DMG, during OAM DMA, the CPU can access only HRAM ($FF80-$FFFE). The programmer must copy a short procedure into HRAM to start the transfer from inside HRAM and wait for completion.

On CGB, cartridge and WRAM are on separate buses. CPU can access ROM/SRAM during OAM DMA from WRAM, or WRAM during OAM DMA from ROM/SRAM. But since call writes return address to stack (usually in WRAM), it's still recommended to busy-wait in HRAM.

**Interrupts**: Critical to prevent interrupts during OAM DMA, especially timer/serial/joypad interrupts not synchronized to LCD. Do this via VBlank handler or di instruction.

While OAM DMA is active, the PPU cannot read OAM properly:
- During Mode 2 (OAM scan): most revisions read each object as off-screen and hidden.
- During Mode 3 (rendering): PPU reads whatever 16-bit word DMA writes to OAM, causing incorrect tile number and attributes.

Most programs execute DMA during Mode 1 (VBlank). Mid-frame DMA is possible for >40 objects but causes lines lacking objects.

metadata.heading_path: OAM DMA Transfer > OAM DMA bus conflicts

text:
## Best practices

Standard 10-byte HRAM routine to start DMA and wait for completion:

```rgbasm
run_dma:
    ld a, HIGH(start address)
    ldh [$FF46], a   ; start DMA transfer
    ld a, 40         ; delay for 4x40 = 160 M-cycles
.wait:
    dec a            ; 1 M-cycle
    jr nz, .wait     ; 3 M-cycles
    ret
```

Compact variant (saves 5 bytes HRAM):
```rgbasm
run_dma:          ; ROM part
    ld a, HIGH(start address)
    ld bc, $2846   ; B: wait time; C: LOW($FF46)
    jp run_dma_tail

run_dma_tail:     ; HRAM part
    ldh [c], a
.wait:
    dec b
    jr nz, .wait
    ret z          ; 1 M-cycle slower, avoids stack read on last DMA cycle
```

For mid-frame transfer: wait for Mode 0 first so the transfer cleanly overlaps Mode 2 on the next two lines.

# Rendering overview

## Terminology

The frame is not drawn atomically; the PPU (Pixel-Processing Unit) draws progressively, directly to the screen. A frame consists of 154 scanlines; during the first 144, the screen is drawn top to bottom, left to right.

Modifying rendering parameters mid-frame creates raster effects (e.g., modifying scroll registers between scanlines for wavy effects).

A dot = one 2^22 Hz (~4.194 MHz) time unit. Dots remain constant regardless of CPU speed mode: 4 dots per Normal Speed M-cycle, 2 per Double Speed M-cycle.

A frame is not exactly 1/60 second: the Game Boy runs slightly slower than 60 Hz (~16.74 ms per frame vs ~16.67 ms, error 0.45%).

## PPU modes

During a frame, the PPU cycles between four modes:

| Mode | Action | Duration | Accessible video memory |
|-----:|--------|----------|------------------------|
| 2 | Searching for OBJs which overlap this line | 80 dots | VRAM, CGB palettes |
| 3 | Sending pixels to the LCD | 172-289 dots (see below) | None |
| 0 | Waiting until end of scanline | 376 - mode 3's duration | VRAM, OAM, CGB palettes |
| 1 | Waiting until next frame (VBlank) | 4560 dots (10 scanlines) | VRAM, OAM, CGB palettes |

While the PPU is accessing video-related memory, that memory is inaccessible to the CPU (writes ignored, reads return garbage usually $FF).

## Mode 3 length

Minimum Mode 3 is 172 dots (160 pixels + 12 dots for initial tile fetches). The Game Boy does not always output pixels steadily; features cause rendering to stall, lengthening Mode 3 and shortening Mode 0 by the same amount.

Three penalties:
- **Background scrolling**: SCX % 8 dots at start of Mode 3 while leftmost tile pixels are discarded.
- **Window**: 6-dot penalty after last non-window pixel while BG fetcher sets up for window.
- **Objects**: Each object drawn (even partially) incurs a 6- to 11-dot penalty.

On DMG and GBC in DMG mode, mid-scanline writes to BGP ($FF47) allow observing this behavior precisely.

### OBJ penalty algorithm

Only the OBJ's leftmost pixel matters. Steps:
1. Determine which tile (BG or window) The Pixel is within.
2. If that tile was not already considered by a previous OBJ: count pixels strictly right of The Pixel in that tile, subtract 2, incur that many dots (or zero if negative).
3. Incur flat 6-dot penalty for fetching the OBJ's tile.

Exception: An OBJ with OAM X position = 0 always incurs an 11-dot penalty regardless of SCX.

# Joypad Input

## FF00 — P1/JOYP: Joypad

The eight Game Boy action/direction buttons are arranged as a 2×4 matrix. Select either action or direction buttons by writing to this register, then read bits 0-3.

Bit layout:
- Bit 5: Select buttons (0 = buttons readable from lower nibble)
- Bit 4: Select d-pad (0 = directional keys readable from lower nibble)
- Bits 3-0: Read-only button states
  - Bit 3: Start / Down
  - Bit 2: Select / Up
  - Bit 1: B / Left
  - Bit 0: A / Right

A button being pressed is seen as the corresponding bit being 0 (not 1). If neither buttons nor d-pad selected ($30 written), low nibble reads $F.

Most programs read this port several times in a row; first reads serve as delay for input stabilization, only last read value is used.

## Usage in SGB software

SGB games misuse the joypad register to output SGB command packets to SNES. SGB programs may also read gamepad states from up to four SNES-connected joypads.

# Hardware Registers
