text:
# Graphics Overview

The Game Boy outputs graphics to a 160×144 pixel LCD using a complex rendering mechanism.

## Tiles

Pixels are grouped in 8×8 squares called tiles (or patterns/characters), the base unit of Game Boy graphics. A tile does not encode color information; instead, it assigns color indices (0-3) to each pixel. This makes Game Boy graphics 2bpp (2 bits per pixel).

When a tile is used in Background or Window, color indices are associated with a palette. When used in an object, indices 1-3 use a palette but ID 0 means transparent.

## Palettes

A palette consists of an array of 4 colors. Modifying palettes enables effects like flashing (damage, invulnerability), fading, and palette swaps.

## Layers

The Game Boy has three layers from back to front: Background, Window, and Objects.

**Background**: Composed of a tilemap — a large grid containing references to tiles. Tiles are reused cheaply in CPU time and memory. The background scrolls as a whole via two hardware registers.

**Window**: A second background layer on top of the background. No transparency; always rectangular; only top-left position controllable. Used for fixed status bars (e.g., Super Mario Land 2).

**Objects**: Made of 1 or 2 stacked tiles (8×8 or 8×16 pixels), displayed anywhere on screen. Multiple objects can be combined as metasprites to draw larger graphical elements.

Summary: A Tile is an 8×8-pixel chunk. An Object is an OAM entry composed of 1-2 tiles, movable independently.

# VRAM Tile Data

Tile data is stored in VRAM at $8000-$97FF; each tile takes 16 bytes, defining 384 tiles. In CGB Mode this doubles to 768 tiles (two VRAM banks).

Each tile has 8×8 pixels with 2 bits per pixel (4 colors/shades). Color 0 is transparent in objects.

Three blocks of 128 tiles:
| Tile IDs for... | Block 0 ($8000-$87FF) | Block 1 ($8800-$8FFF) | Block 2 ($9000-$97FF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Objects | 0-127 | 128-255 | — |
| BG/Win, LCDC.4=1 | 0-127 | 128-255 | — |
| BG/Win, LCDC.4=0 | — | 128-255 | 0-127 |

**$8000 method**: Unsigned addressing; tiles 0-127 in block 0, tiles 128-255 in block 1.
**$8800 method**: Signed addressing; tiles 0-127 from block 2, tiles 128-255 (i.e., -128 to -1) from block 1.

Objects always use $8000 addressing. BG/Window mode controlled by LCDC bit 4.

## Data format

Each tile occupies 16 bytes; each row is represented by 2 bytes (topmost line first).

For each row, the first byte specifies the least significant bit of each pixel's color ID, and the second byte specifies the most significant bit. In both bytes, bit 7 = leftmost pixel, bit 0 = rightmost.

Example: Row data $3C $7E = binary 00111100 and 01111110. Leftmost bits are 0+0 → color index 0. Next bits 0+1 → color index 2 (MSB first!). Full row evaluates to: 0 2 3 3 3 3 2 0.

Each pixel has a color index of 0-3, translated by palettes (BGP $FF47, OBP0/OBP1 $FF48/$FF49 for DMG; BCPS/BCPD/OCPS/OCPD for CGB). In OBJs, color index 0 is transparent.

keywords: LSB byte first MSB byte second

text:
# Accessing VRAM and OAM

**Warning**: When the PPU is drawing the screen, it reads directly from VRAM and OAM. During these periods, the CPU cannot access them.

Writes to VRAM/OAM during PPU access are ignored (data unchanged). Reads return undefined data (typically $FF).

The program should verify if VRAM/OAM is accessible before reading/writing, usually by checking Mode bits in STAT ($FF41). When doing so, ensure no interrupts occur between the wait loop and the memory access; accessibility is guaranteed only for a few cycles after a wait loop exits.

## VRAM ($8000-$9FFF) is accessible during Modes 0-2

Mode 0 (HBlank), Mode 1 (VBlank), and Mode 2 (OAM search) allow VRAM access.

Typical wait procedure:
```rgbasm
    ld hl, $FF41   ; STAT Register
.wait:
    bit 1, [hl]    ; Wait until Mode is 0 or 1
    jr nz, .wait
```

Even at end of Mode 0/1, VRAM access is safe because the next period is Mode 2 (also allows VRAM). But be careful about STAT interrupts that could return during Mode 3.

In CGB Mode, HDMA ($FF51-$FF55) provides an alternate method to write VRAM data.

Alternative synchronization: Enable only Mode 0 STAT interrupt (STAT bit 3), enable STAT in IE (bit 1), execute di, then halt. This uses entire Mode 0 + following Mode 2 (165-288 dots total).

metadata.heading_path: Accessing VRAM and OAM > VRAM ($8000-$9FFF) is accessible during Modes 0-2

text:
## OAM ($FE00-$FE9F) is accessible during Modes 0-1

Mode 0 (HBlank) and Mode 1 (VBlank) allow OAM access.

During those modes, OAM can be accessed directly or via DMA transfer ($FF46). Outside those modes, DMA out-prioritizes the PPU in accessing OAM; the PPU reads $FF from OAM during that time.

Typical wait procedure:
```rgbasm
    ld hl, $FF41   ; STAT Register
.waitNotBlank:
    bit 1, [hl]
    jr z, .waitNotBlank   ; Wait until mode is NOT 0 or 1
.waitBlank:
    bit 1, [hl]
    jr nz, .waitBlank     ; Wait until Mode 0 or 1 begins
```

The two wait loops ensure Mode 0 (and Mode 1 at frame end) lasts long enough after the procedure. For VBlank waiting, a STAT interrupt is better. DMA transfer is more efficient than direct OAM writes.

While display is disabled, both VRAM and OAM are fully accessible.

metadata.heading_path: Accessing VRAM and OAM > OAM ($FE00-$FE9F) is accessible during Modes 0-1

text:
# Viewport position (Scrolling)

## FF42–FF43 — SCY, SCX: Background viewport Y position, X position

These registers can be accessed even during Mode 3, but modifications may not take effect immediately.

SCY and SCX specify the top-left coordinates of the visible 160×144 pixel area within the 256×256 pixel BG map. Values range 0-255.

The PPU calculates bottom-right viewport coordinates: bottom = (SCY + 143) % 256, right = (SCX + 159) % 256. Values larger than 255 wrap around toward the top-left corner of the tilemap.

## Mid-frame behavior

Scroll registers are re-read on each tile fetch, except for the low 3 bits of SCX, which are only read at scanline start (for initial pixel shifting).

All models before CGB-D read the Y coordinate once per bitplane (allowing desync with precisely timed SCY writes). CGB-D and later use the same Y coordinate for both bitplanes.

## FF4A–FF4B — WY, WX: Window Y position, X position plus 7

These registers specify the on-screen coordinates of the Window's top-left pixel.

The Window is visible (if enabled) when WX and WY are in range [0; 166] and [0; 143] respectively. Values WX=7, WY=0 place the Window at top-left, completely covering the background.

## Window mid-frame behavior

For least glitchy results, only write to WX, WY, and LCDC during VBlank. If mid-frame writes are required, prefer HBlank.

To hide the Window for part of the screen (e.g., top status bar), set WX to a high value rather than writing to LCDC.

### Window rendering criteria

The PPU tracks a Y condition throughout a frame:
- On each VBlank, the Y condition is cleared (false).
- At scanline start, if WY equals LY, the Y condition becomes true (remains so for subsequent scanlines).

On GBC, clearing Window enable bit in LCDC resets the Y condition; WY must be set to LY or greater for the Window to display again.

The PPU maintains a counter initialized to 0 at scanline start, incremented per pixel rendered. It also increments 7 times before first pixel (covering fine scroll discard).

When counter equals WX, if Y condition is true and Window enable bit set in LCDC, background rendering resets from the active row of Window's tilemap.

- This can happen more than once per scanline (requires disabling Window via LCDC first).
- If not triggered, Window's tilemap Y coordinate doesn't increase; hiding then showing produces vertical striped stretching.
- WX=0: Window switches before fine scroll adjustment, shifted left by SCX % 8 pixels.
- On monochrome systems, WX=166 has a bug: Window spans entire screen but offset vertically by one scanline.
- On monochrome systems, if Window disabled via LCDC but conditions met and would start exactly on BG tile boundary, a single pixel with ID 0 is inserted, offsetting the remainder of the scanline.

keywords: WX WY mid-frame write VBlank HBlank
GBC LCDC window enable reset Y condition
LCDC disabled tile boundary pixel ID 0

text:
# Pixel FIFO

## Introduction

FIFO (First In, First Out): first pixel pushed is first popped. There are two independent pixel FIFOs — one for background pixels and one for object (sprite) pixels.

Each FIFO holds up to 16 pixels. The FIFO and Pixel Fetcher ensure at least 8 pixels are always present (required for Pixel Rendering). Each FIFO is manipulated only during Mode 3.

Each pixel in the FIFO has four properties:
- Color: value 0-3
- Palette: CGB value 0-7; DMG applies only to objects
- Sprite Priority: CGB = OAM index; DMG doesn't exist
- Background Priority: value of OBJ-to-BG Priority bit

## FIFO Pixel Fetcher

The fetcher fetches a row of 8 background/window pixels. It has 5 steps: first four take 2 dots each, fifth is attempted every dot until success.

Steps: Get Tile → Get Tile Data Low → Get Tile Data High → Sleep → Push

### Get Tile

Determines which BG/Window tile to fetch from. Default tilemap is $9800.
- When LCDC.3 enabled and X not in window: tilemap $9C00 used.
- When LCDC.6 enabled and X inside window: tilemap $9C00 used.

Fetcher tracks X/Y coordinates:
- Window tile: uses window X coordinate directly.
- BG tile: X = ((SCX / 8) + fetcherX) & $1F (range 0-31).
- Y for BG: (currentScanline + SCY) & 255 (range 0-255).

If VRAM access is blocked, tile value reads as $FF. CGB can access both tile index and attributes in the same clock dot.

metadata.heading_path: Pixel FIFO > FIFO Pixel Fetcher > Get Tile

text:
### Get Tile Data Low

Check LCDC.4 for tilemap. CGB also checks VRAM bank and vertical flip. Retrieves tile data from VRAM (reads $FF if blocked).

### Get Tile Data High

Same as Low except address incremented by 1. Also pushes a row of BG/Window pixels to FIFO (3 total push chances per complete fetcher cycle).

### Push

Pushes 8 pixels to background FIFO only if empty. Horizontal flip determines LSB-first vs MSB-first push order.

### Sleep

Do nothing.

### VRAM Access

VRAM read blocked (returns $FF) during: LCD turning off, scanline 0 on CGB not double speed, mode 3→0 transition, CGB OAM search index 37.

VRAM read restored at: scanline 0 DMG/CGB double speed, DMG OAM search index 37, mode 2→3 transition. Conditions checked only when entering STOP; always restored leaving STOP.

metadata.section: Get Tile Data Low/High, Push, Sleep, VRAM Access
metadata.heading_path: Pixel FIFO > FIFO Pixel Fetcher > Get Tile Data / Push / VRAM Access
keywords: VRAM blocked LCD off scanline 0 mode transition OAM index 37

text:
## Mode 3 Operation

At beginning of Mode 3, both BG and OAM FIFOs are cleared.

### The Window

When rendering window: BG FIFO cleared, fetcher reset to step 1. WX=0 with SCX & 7 > 0 shortens mode 3 by 1 dot.

Bug: Changing WX mid-scanline after window started rendering pushes a color-0 lowest-priority pixel when new WX value is reached again.

### Sprites

For each object on current scanline (if LCDC.1 enabled — ignored on CGB):
- Fetcher advances one step until at step 5 or BG FIFO not empty (+1 dot per advance). May be canceled.
- SCX & 7 > 0 with object at X=0 lengthens mode 3 by lower 3 bits of SCX dots. Timing unconfirmed.
- After checking X=0, fetcher advances two steps (first +1 dot, second +3 dots).
- Lower address retrieved (+1 dot). Upper address retrieved (no penalty).
- VRAM Access checked for both addresses.
- If OAM FIFO has <8 pixels, transparent lowest-priority pixels pushed.
- Each pixel checked: on CGB horizontal flip checked here. Non-white target replaces white OAM pixel, or higher priority replaces lower.

metadata.heading_path: Pixel FIFO > Mode 3 Operation > Window / Sprites
keywords: Window BG FIFO clear fetcher reset step 1

text:
### Pixel Rendering

BG and OAM FIFOs are mixed. If both have pixels, one popped from each.

If OAM pixel not transparent and LCDC.1 enabled: OAM pixel's BG priority property is used if same or higher than BG pixel's BG priority.

Pixels won't push to LCD if BG FIFO empty or pixel >= 160.

LCDC.0 disabled: BG disabled on DMG, no BG priority on CGB. Disabled BG pixel color = 0. Non-zero BG pixel with priority discards object pixel.

On DMG: BG pixel color from BGP register pushed to LCD. On CGB when palette access blocked: black pixel pushed.

Object pixel priority: Color from OAM FIFO popped pixel. DMG uses OBP1 (palette=1) or OBP0 (palette=0). CGB palette blocked → black pixel.

### CGB Palette Access

Blocked (black pixel): LCD off, first HBlank frame, OAM search index 37, mode 2→3 transition, entering HBlank not double speed (blocked 2 dots later).

Restored: End of mode 2, 2 dots entering HBlank in double speed. Checked only when entering STOP; always restored leaving STOP.

metadata.section: Pixel Rendering and CGB Palette Access
metadata.heading_path: Pixel FIFO > Mode 3 Operation > Pixel Rendering / CGB Palette Access
keywords: LCDC.0 disabled DMG no priority CGB
HBlank first frame OAM index 37 mode transition

text:
### Object Fetch Canceling

Object fetching may be canceled if LCDC.1 is disabled while PPU fetches an object from OAM.

Canceling lengthens Mode 3 by: dots of previous instruction + residual dots for PPU processing.

When OAM fetching is canceled: a pixel is rendered, and the fetcher advances one step (+1 dot if current pixel < 160). At pixel 160, PPU stops processing objects (not visible).

metadata.heading_path: Pixel FIFO > Mode 3 Operation > Object Fetch Canceling
keywords: Mode 3 lengthen previous instruction dots residual

text:
# OAM Corruption Bug

A hardware flaw causes rubbish data written to OAM when certain instructions are used while their 16-bit content (before operation) is in range $FE00-$FEFF and PPU is in Mode 2:

Affected instructions: inc rr, dec rr (rr = bc, de, hl); ld a,[hli], ld a,[hld]; ld [hli],a, ld [hld],a.

Objects 0 and 1 ($FE00 & $FE04) are not affected. Game Boy Color and Advance are not affected, even running monochrome software.

keywords: inc rr dec rr bc de hl

text:
## Accurate Description

The OAM Bug consists of two different bugs:
1. Reading/writing OAM (including $FEA0-$FEFF) during Mode 2 corrupts it.
2. Increment/decrement on any 16-bit register (BC, DE, HL, SP, PC) while in OAM range ($FE00-$FEFF) triggers an access because the CPU's IDU is directly tied to the address bus.

## Affected Operations

- Any memory access instruction accessing OAM.
- inc rr, dec rr: if 16-bit register points to OAM, triggers write corruption.
- ld [hli],a etc.: trigger corruption twice (memory access + inc/dec).
- pop rr, ret family: bug triggers 3 times (one read, one glitched write, another read without glitched write).
- push rr, call family, rst xx, interrupt handling: 4 triggers (two writes + two glitched dec sp writes; effectively 3 writes since one overlaps).
- Executing code from OAM: PC inside OAM ($FF = rst $38) triggers twice. Multi-byte opcode from $FDFF/$FDFE similarly triggers twice per read.

metadata.heading_path: OAM Corruption Bug > Accurate Description / Affected Operations
keywords: IDU increment decrement unit address bus tied
push call rst interrupt 4 triggers dec sp

text:
## Corruption Patterns

OAM is split into 20 rows of 8 bytes. During Mode 2, PPU reads rows consecutively (one per M-cycle). OAM uses a 16-bit data bus; all operations are on 16-bit words.

### Write Corruption

Corrupts the currently accessed row (except first row with objects 0-1):
- First word replaced: ((a ^ c) & (b ^ c)) ^ c, where a = original value, b = first word preceding row, c = third word preceding row.
- Last three words copied from last three words of preceding row.

### Read Corruption

Same as write but expression is: b | (a & c).

### Write During Increase/Decrease

Two writes in one M-cycle behave like a single write.

### Read During Increase/Decrease

Triggers both read AND write. Does not affect first four rows or last row:
- First word of preceding row replaced: (b & (a | c | d)) | (a & c & d), where a = first word two rows before, b = first word preceding row, c = first word current row, d = third word preceding row.
- Preceding row contents copied to current row and two rows before. Then normal read corruption applied.

keywords: write corruption XOR formula a^c b^c
read corruption OR AND formula b|a&c

text:
# VRAM Tile Maps

The Game Boy contains two 32×32 tile maps in VRAM at $9800-$9BFF and $9C00-$9FFF. Any of these can display the Background or Window.

## Tile Indexes

Each map contains 1-byte indexes of tiles to display. Tiles are obtained from Tile Data using either addressing mode (selected via LCDC register). Since one tile is 8×8 pixels, each map holds a 256×256 pixel picture; only 160×144 displayed on LCD at any time.

## BG Map Attributes (CGB Mode only)

In CGB Mode, an additional 32×32 byte map is stored in VRAM Bank 1 (each byte defines attributes for the corresponding tile-number map entry in VRAM Bank 0):

Bit layout:
- Bit 7: Priority (0 = No; 1 = BG/Window color indices 1-3 drawn over OBJ regardless of OBJ priority)
- Bit 6: Y flip (0 = Normal; 1 = Vertically mirrored)
- Bit 5: X flip (0 = Normal; 1 = Horizontally mirrored)
- Bit 4: Ignored by hardware
- Bit 3: Bank (0 = VRAM bank 0; 1 = VRAM bank 1)
- Bits 2-0: Color palette (BGP0-7)

### BG-to-OBJ Priority in CGB Mode

Priority between BG and OBJ is declared in three places: BG Map Attribute bit 7, LCDC bit 0, OAM Attributes bit 7.

Rules:
- If BG color index = 0: OBJ always has priority.
- If LCDC bit 0 clear: OBJ always has priority.
- If both BG attr and OAM attr bit 7 clear: OBJ has priority.
- Otherwise: BG has priority (for colors 1-3; color 0 still OBJ).

Note: OAM Attributes bit 7 grants OBJ priority when CLEAR, not SET. Priority between all OBJs is resolved before BG layer priority.

metadata.section: BG Map Attributes and BG-to-OBJ Priority
metadata.heading_path: VRAM Tile Maps > BG Map Attributes (CGB Mode only) / BG-to-OBJ Priority
keywords: CGB Mode VRAM Bank 1 attribute map
OAM attr bit 7 clear OBJ priority

text:
## Background (BG)

SCY and SCX registers scroll the Background, specifying origin of visible 160×144 area within total 256×256 BG map. Visible area wraps around the map.

In Non-CGB mode, Background (and Window) can be disabled using LCDC bit 0.

## Window

The Window overlays the Background. Content is not scrollable; always displayed from top-left tile of its tilemap. Position adjusted via WX and WY registers; top-left corner at screen coordinates (WX-7, WY).

Window display toggled by LCDC bit 5. In Non-CGB mode, this only works if LCDC bit 0 is set.
Enabling Window makes Mode 3 slightly longer on visible scanlines.

**Window Internal Line Counter**: The window keeps an internal line counter similar to LY, incrementing alongside it. It only increments when the window is visible (per Window rendering criteria). This determines what window line renders on each scanline.

keywords: SCY SCX scroll 160x144 visible 256x256 map wrap
LCDC bit 0 disable BG Window Non-CGB

text:
# Audio Overview

Game Boy audio has four sound generation units called channels 1-4 (CH1-CH4). Each channel is specialized differently.

Channels 1 and 2: Pulse channels producing pulse width modulated waves with 4 fixed duty settings.
Channel 3: Wave channel producing arbitrary user-supplied waves from Wave RAM.
Channel 4: Noise channel producing pseudo-random wave via LFSR.
VIN channel: Analog signal from cartridge for external hardware (no licensed games used it; omitted from GBA).

Each channel has components:
| Channel | Sweep | Frequency | Wave Form | Length Timer | Volume |
|---------|-------|-----------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Square 1 | Sweep | Period Counter | Duty | Length Timer | Envelope |
| Square 2 | — | Period Counter | Duty | Length Timer | Envelope |
| Wave | — | Period Counter | Wave | Length Timer | Volume |
| Noise | — | Period Counter | LFSR | Length Timer | Envelope |

keywords: CH2 pulse duty envelope no sweep

text:
## Common concepts

### APU
The sound chip (APU) runs off the same Master Clock as CPU and PPU. SGB1 runs ~2.4% faster (higher pitch); SGB2 rectifies this. APU tick length is not affected by CGB double speed.

All interfaces use durations instead of frequencies. E.g., "256 Hz tick" = 1/256th second.

### Triggering
Triggering a channel turns it on (if DAC not off) and starts playing from beginning. Most parameter changes take effect immediately, but some require re-triggering.

### Volume & envelope
Master volume control has separate left/right settings. Each channel's volume individually set. Envelope for CH1/CH2/CH4: initial volume, direction (not slope), duration. All envelopes tick at 64 Hz; volume changes every 1-7 ticks.

### Length timer
All channels can auto-shutdown after a set time. If enabled, length timer ticks at 256 Hz from initial value. Reaches 64 (CH1/2/4) or 256 (CH3) → channel off.

### Frequency
APU works with periods (negative values), not frequencies. Higher period value = lower period = higher frequency.

metadata.section: Common concepts: APU, Triggering, Envelope, Length, Frequency
keywords: APU Master Clock SGB1 2.4% faster CGB double speed unaffected
envelope 64 Hz tick CH1 CH2 CH4 initial volume direction duration
length timer 256 Hz CH1-4 threshold 64 CH3 threshold 256
period negative value higher register lower period higher frequency

text:
# Audio Registers

Registers follow NRxy naming: x = channel (1-4) or 5 for global, y = register ID within channel. NRx0 = channel feature, NRx1 = length timer, NRx2 = volume/envelope, NRx3 = period low, NRx4 = trigger/length enable/period high.

### FF26 — NR52: Audio master control
- Bit 7: Audio on/off. Turning APU off drains ~16% less power but clears all registers (read-only until back on except NR52). Does NOT affect Wave RAM or DIV-APU counter.
- Bits 3-0: CH4/3/2/1 on? (Read-only status; writing does NOT enable/disable channels).

Channel turns off when: length timer expires, CH1 period sweep overflows, or DAC turned off. Envelope reaching volume 0 does NOT turn channel off.

### FF25 — NR51: Sound panning
Bits 7-4: CH4/3/2/1 left output. Bits 3-0: CH4/3/2/1 right output. Setting bit = 1 enables channel to that output. Selecting/deselecting with DAC enabled causes audio pop.

### FF24 — NR50: Master volume & VIN panning
Bit 7: VIN left, Bit 3: VIN right (set to 0 if no external hardware). Bits 6-4: Left volume, Bits 2-0: Right volume. Value 0 = volume 1 (quietest), value 7 = volume 8 (no reduction). Amplifier never mutes non-silent input.

metadata.section: NRxy naming and Global control NR50-NR52
keywords: $FF26 NR52 audio on/off bit 7 power save clear registers CH1-4 status bits read-only
$FF25 NR51 sound panning left right CH1-CH4 audio pop DAC
$FF24 NR50 master volume VIN panning bits 6-4 left bits 2-0 right value 0=quietest 7=max never mute

text:
### FF10 — NR10: Channel 1 sweep
Controls CH1's period sweep. Bit layout:
- Bits 6-4: Pace (how often sweep iterations happen, in 128 Hz ticks / 7.8 ms). Value 0 disables iterations instantly.
- Bit 3: Direction (0 = Addition/period increases; 1 = Subtraction/period decreases)
- Bits 2-0: Individual step — new period L(t+1) = Lt ± Lt/(2^step)

On each iteration, the period in NR13/NR14 is modified and written back. In addition mode, if period > $7FF, channel turns off (even with pace=0). Period 0 cannot be changed by sweep; sweep cannot underflow.

### FF11 — NR11: Channel 1 length timer & duty cycle
- Bits 7-6: Wave duty (00 = 12.5%, 01 = 25%, 10 = 50%, 11 = 75%). No audible difference between 25% and 75%.
- Bits 5-0: Initial length timer (higher value = shorter time before channel cut).

metadata.section: CH1 NR10 sweep and NR11 duty cycle
metadata.heading_path: Audio Registers > Sound Channel 1 — Pulse with period sweep > NR10 / NR11
keywords: $FF10 NR10 CH1 sweep bits 6-4 pace 128Hz bit 3 direction add/subtract bits 2-0 step formula overflow $7FF off
$FF11 NR11 CH1 length timer duty cycle bits 7-6 12.5% 25% 50% 75% bits 5-0 initial length

text:
### FF12 — NR12: Channel 1 volume & envelope
Controls digital amplitude and sweep applied to it.
- Bits 7-4: Initial volume (readable but NOT updated by envelope)
- Bit 3: Env dir (0 = decrease; 1 = increase)
- Bits 2-0: Sweep pace (envelope ticks at 64 Hz; changes every N ticks). Value 0 disables envelope.

Setting bits 3-7 all to 0 turns DAC off. Writes while channel is on require retriggering.

### FF13 — NR13: Channel 1 period low [write-only]
Low 8 bits of 11-bit period value. Upper 3 bits in low 3 bits of NR14.

Period divider is an up counter clocked at 1048576 Hz (once per 4 dots). Waveform is 8 samples long.
Tone frequency = 131072 / (2048 - period_value) Hz. Period value treated as negative number in 11-bit two's complement.

Period changes only take effect after current sample ends.

### FF14 — NR14: Channel 1 period high & control
- Bit 7: Trigger (enables CH1, resets length/period divider/envelope/volume/sweep)
- Bit 6: Length enable (takes effect immediately)
- Bits 2-0: Period upper 3 bits

metadata.section: CH1 NR12 volume envelope and NR13-NR14 period control
metadata.heading_path: Audio Registers > Sound Channel 1 — Pulse with period sweep > NR12 / NR13 / NR14
keywords: $FF12 NR12 CH1 volume bits 7-4 initial bit 3 envelope direction bits 2-0 pace 64Hz DAC off all zero retrigger required
$FF13 NR13 CH1 period low write-only 11-bit two's complement negative 1048576 Hz clock tone frequency formula delay sample end
$FF14 NR14 CH1 trigger bit 7 length enable bit 6 period high bits 2-0

text:
## Sound Channel 2 — Pulse
Works exactly like CH1 except no period sweep (no NR10 equivalent).
Register mapping: NR21 ($FF16) → NR11, NR22 ($FF17) → NR12, NR23 ($FF18) → NR13, NR24 ($FF19) → NR14.

## Sound Channel 3 — Wave output
Allows arbitrary waveforms from Wave RAM (32 samples, 4-bit each). Can be shortened with repeating patterns or artificially lengthened for sample playback.

### FF1A — NR30: Channel 3 DAC enable
Bit 7: DAC on/off. Turning off immediately turns channel off. Often turned off before writing to Wave RAM. May cause audio pop.

### FF1B — NR31: Channel 3 length timer [write-only]
Bits 7-0: Initial length timer (higher = shorter time).

### FF1C — NR32: Channel 3 output level
No envelope; coarser volume control:
- Bits 6-5 = 00: Mute
- 01: 100% volume
- 10: 50% (shift right once)
- 11: 25% (shift right twice)

metadata.section: CH2 reference and CH3 overview NR30-NR32
metadata.heading_path: Audio Registers > Sound Channel 2 / Sound Channel 3 — Wave output > NR30-NR32
keywords: $FF16 NR21 $FF17 NR22 $FF18 NR23 $FF19 NR24 CH2 pulse no sweep same as CH1
$FF1A NR30 CH3 DAC enable bit 7 audio pop Wave RAM write safe
$FF1B NR31 CH3 length timer write-only bits 7-0
$FF1C NR32 CH3 output level bits 6-5 mute 100% 50% 25% no envelope

text:
### FF1D — NR33: Channel 3 period low [write-only]
Low 8 bits of 11-bit period value. Upper 3 bits in NR34.

Wave channel period divider clocked at 2097152 Hz (once per 2 dots). Waveform is 32 samples long.
Tone frequency = 65536 / (2048 - period_value) Hz. Same period value produces half the pulse channel frequency (one octave lower).

Period changes take effect after next wave RAM read.

### FF1E — NR34: Channel 3 period high & control
- Bit 7: Trigger (enables CH3, resets length/period divider/volume; resets Wave RAM index but NOT refills it)
- Bit 6: Length enable
- Bits 2-0: Period upper 3 bits

Retriggering CH3 while about to read wave RAM on monochrome consoles corrupts wave RAM unpredictably.
Triggering does not immediately start playing; last sample (reset to 0 when APU off) outputs until next read.

### FF30–FF3F — Wave pattern RAM
16 bytes, each holding two 4-bit samples. CH3 reads left-to-right, upper nibble first. First sample read on start is index 1 (lower nibble of $FF30), NOT upper nibble.

Accessing wave RAM while CH3 active causes misbehavior: AGB returns $FF/ignores writes; monochrome only accessible on same cycle as CH3; other consoles access whatever byte CH3 reads. Wave RAM accessible normally if DAC on but channel not active.

metadata.section: CH3 NR33-NR34 period and Wave RAM
metadata.heading_path: Audio Registers > Sound Channel 3 — Wave output > NR33 / NR34 / Wave RAM
keywords: $FF1D NR33 CH3 period low write-only 2097152 Hz clock tone frequency formula delay wave RAM read
$FF1E NR34 CH3 trigger bit 7 length enable bit 6 period high bits 2-0 retrigger corruption monochrome playback delay last sample
$FF30-$FF3F Wave RAM 16 bytes 32 samples 4-bit first sample index 1 lower nibble AGB locked $FF monochrome same-cycle access

text:
## Sound Channel 4 — Noise
Outputs white noise by randomly switching amplitude between two levels. Frequency adjustable for harder/softer sound. LFSR settings can make output more regular (closer to pulse than noise).

### FF20 — NR41: Channel 4 length timer [write-only]
Bits 5-0: Initial length timer (higher = shorter time).

### FF21 — NR42: Channel 4 volume & envelope
Functions exactly like NR12 ($FF12). Refer to CH1 documentation.

### FF22 — NR43: Channel 4 frequency & randomness
- Bits 7-4: Clock shift. Shift = 14 or 15 stops clocking entirely.
- Bit 3: LFSR width (0 = 15-bit; 1 = 7-bit, more regular). Switching 15→7 bit in certain state locks LFSR (silences CH4); retrigger to reset.
- Bits 2-0: Clock divider. Divider = 0 treated as 0.5.

LFSR clock frequency = 262144 / (divider × 2^shift) Hz. If shifted-out bit is 0, output 0; otherwise outputs NR42 volume.

### FF23 — NR44: Channel 4 control
- Bit 7: Trigger (enables CH4, resets length/envelope/volume/LFSR)
- Bit 6: Length enable

metadata.heading_path: Audio Registers > Sound Channel 4 — Noise > NR41-NR44
keywords: $FF20 NR41 CH4 length timer bits 5-0 write-only
$FF21 NR42 CH4 volume envelope same as NR12 $FF12
$FF22 NR43 CH4 frequency randomness bits 7-4 clock shift bit 3 LFSR width 15-bit 7-bit lockup retrigger bits 2-0 divider 262144 Hz formula shift 14-15 stop
$FF23 NR44 CH4 trigger bit 7 length enable bit 6 reset LFSR

text:
# Audio Details

The APU is a collection of counters. Each of the four conceptual channels has a generation circuit and a DAC. The digital value ($0-$F, 0-15) is linearly translated by the DAC into an analog value between -1 and 1.

The four analog outputs are fed into the mixer, which selectively adds them (depending on NR51) into two analog outputs (Left/Right). Range of these outputs: -4 to 4. Both get volume scaled from NR50 and then from the hardware volume knob. The former never mutes a non-silent input; the latter can.

Each output goes through a high-pass filter (HPF) that constantly tries to pull the signal towards analog 0, removing DC offset from inactive channels with enabled DACs and off-center waveforms.

## PCM Registers (CGB Mode only)

Two undocumented registers allow reading generation circuit outputs directly:
- $FF76 — PCM12: Low nibble = CH1 digital output; high nibble = CH2 digital output. Read-only.
- $FF77 — PCM34: Same for channels 3 and 4. Read-only.

keywords: APU counters DAC digital $0-$F analog -1 to 1
mixer NR51 left right output range -4 to 4
NR50 volume scaling never mutes non-silent
high-pass filter HPF DC offset removal
$FF76 PCM12 CH1 CH2 digital output CGB read-only
$FF77 PCM34 CH3 CH4 digital output CGB read-only

text:
## DIV-APU Counter

A DIV-APU counter increments every time DIV's bit 4 (bit 5 in double-speed mode) goes from 1 to 0, at a frequency of 512 Hz regardless of double-speed. Writing to DIV while the relevant bit is set triggers the falling edge, making the counter increase faster.

Events per N DIV-APU ticks:
| Event | Rate | Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| Envelope sweep | 8 | 64 Hz |
| Sound length | 2 | 256 Hz |
| CH1 freq sweep | 4 | 128 Hz |

## Mixer and HPF Details

The HPF removes constant biases over time, eliminating DC offset from inactive channels with enabled DACs and off-center waveforms.

**Avoiding audio pops**: Enabling/disabling a DAC, adding/removing via NR51, or changing volume in NR50 causes an audio pop (DC offset change smoothed by HPFs over time). To avoid: write $08 to NRx2 (silences channel but keeps DAC on), then $80 to NRx4 to retrigger and reload NRx2.

HPF aggressiveness varies by model: GBA > GBC > DMG. More aggressive = faster pull toward analog 0, which also distorts waveforms more.

keywords: DIV-APU counter 512 Hz DIV bit 4 double-speed bit 5 write trigger
envelope sweep 64 Hz sound length 256 Hz CH1 freq sweep 128 Hz
HPF high-pass filter DC offset removal
audio pop DAC NR51 NR50 avoid $08 NRx2 $80 NRx4 retrigger
HPF aggressiveness GBC > DMG GBA most aggressive waveform distortion

text:
## DACs

Channel x's DAC is enabled iff `[NRx2] & $F8 != 0`. Exception: CH3's DAC controlled directly by bit 7 of NR30. Envelope changes volume but not the stored NRx2 value, so doesn't disable DACs.

When enabled: digital $0-$F linearly translated to analog -1 to 1 with NEGATIVE slope (digital 0 maps to analog 1). When disabled: fades to analog 0 (= digital 7.5); fade nature varies between models and is not fully deterministic.

NR52's low 4 bits report whether channels are on, NOT their DACs.

## Channels Activation/Deactivation

A channel activates via write to NRx4's MSB (trigger), unless its DAC is off — then it stays disabled. A disabled channel outputs digital 0, which an enabled DAC converts to analog 1.

Channel deactivation methods:
- Turning off its DAC
- Length timer expiring
- CH1 only: frequency sweep overflowing the period

keywords: NRx2 $F8 DAC enable check NR30 bit 7 CH3 exception
negative slope digital 0 analog 1 disabled fade analog 0 digital 7.5
NR52 low bits channel status not DAC status
trigger NRx4 MSB DAC off forces disable
deactivation DAC off length timer sweep overflow

text:
## Pulse Channel with Sweep (CH1) Detailed Behavior

CH1's frequency sweep unit (NR10) has a sweep timer, enabled flag, and period shadow register. The sweep timer is clocked at 128 Hz by DIV-APU.

**Trigger event for CH1:**
- Period value copied to shadow register
- Sweep timer reset
- Enabled flag set if sweep pace OR individual step are non-zero; cleared otherwise
- If individual step non-zero: frequency calculation AND overflow check performed immediately

**Frequency calculation**: Take shadow register value, shift right by individual step, optionally negate (per direction bit), sum with shadow register to produce new frequency.

**Overflow check**: New frequency > 2047 ($7FF) → CH1 disabled.

**Sweep timer clocked** (when enabled flag set and pace non-zero): Calculate new frequency + overflow check. If ≤ 2047 and step non-zero: write to shadow register AND NR13/NR14, then run calculation+overflow AGAIN immediately with the new value (second result NOT written back).

Modifying CH1 frequency via NR13/NR14 while sweep active doesn't affect shadow register; next sweep update overwrites it. Re-triggering avoids this.

metadata.heading_path: Audio Details > Pulse channel with sweep (CH1)
keywords: CH1 NR10 sweep timer 128 Hz DIV-APU shadow register enabled flag
trigger copy period reset timer set flag immediate calculation overflow check
frequency calculation shift right step negate direction sum shadow register
sweep clocked write back NR13 NR14 second pass not written
NR13 NR14 modification lost next sweep re-trigger preserves

text:
## Pulse Channels (CH1, CH2) Duty Step

Each pulse channel has an internal duty step counter indexing into the selected waveform. The duty step increments at 8× the channel's frequency. Cannot be reset except by turning APU off. Retriggering resets the duty step timer; retriggering often enough prevents it from advancing.

First output of a newly started pulse channel is always digital zero.

## Wave Channel (CH3) Sample Index

CH3 has an internal sample index counter incrementing at 32× the channel's frequency. Each increment reads the corresponding nibble from wave RAM. Sample #0 is skipped on startup.

CH3 stores each read sample in a buffer and emits it continuously. Retriggering does NOT clear this buffer — last sample ever read repeats. Buffer IS cleared when APU turns on (emits digital 0).

Output level control shifts the DIGITAL value, not analog. Mid-playback changes bias toward 0 → analog biased toward 1; HPF smooths over time.

## Noise Channel (CH4) LFSR

CH4 uses a 16-bit LFSR: 15 bits state + 1 bit for next shift-in value.

On each tick:
1. XOR of bit 0 and bit 1 written to bit 15 (1 if identical, 0 otherwise)
2. In short mode (7-bit): bit 15 also copied to bit 7
3. LFSR shifted right; bit 0 selects between 0 and chosen volume

LFSR set to 0 on trigger.

**Lock-up**: If active portion of LFSR contains only 1s, only 1s are generated — CH4 never changes (silenced until retriggered). Can occur by switching from 15-bit to 7-bit mode when bottom 7 bits are all 1s.

metadata.section: Pulse duty step, Wave sample index, Noise LFSR
metadata.heading_path: Audio Details > Pulse channels / Wave channel / Noise channel

CH3 sample index 32x frequency wave RAM nibble sample 0 skipped buffer not cleared trigger cleared APU on
output level shift digital not analog mid-playback bias HPF smooth

text:
## Game Boy Advance Audio

The APU was heavily reworked for GBA:
- Mixing done digitally instead of by analog circuitry; sound converted to analog signal with offset added (see SOUNDBIAS in GBATEK).
- No physical DACs. Emulated digitally: a disabled DAC behaves like an enabled DAC receiving 0 as input.
- CH3's DAC output is INVERTED. Causes a loud spike when disabled — disconnect channel via NR51 before accessing wave RAM.

None of the additional GBA features (more wave RAM, digital FIFOs) are available to CGB programs.

metadata.heading_path: Audio Details > Game Boy Advance audio
keywords: GBA APU reworked digital mixing SOUNDBIAS GBATEK
no physical DACs emulated disabled = enabled with 0 input
CH3 DAC output inverted loud spike disconnect NR51 wave RAM access
CGB programs no additional GBA features

text:
## Obscure Audio Behavior

- Volume envelope and sweep timers treat a period of 0 as 8.
- After power-on, first duty step of CH1/CH2 after initial trigger plays as if it were 0. Duty cycle clocking disabled until first trigger.
- Triggering CH3: first sample played is the previous one in high nibble of buffer; next sample is second nibble from wave table (first byte not loaded on trigger). First nibble not played until waveform loops.
- Triggering CH1/CH2: low two bits of frequency timer NOT modified.
- Extra length clocking: writing to NRx4 when DIV-APU next step doesn't clock the length timer. If length was previously disabled, now enabled, and timer non-zero → decremented. If decrement makes zero and trigger clear → channel disabled. CGB-02 differs (only needs prior disable); fixed on CGB-04/CGB-05. Breaks Prehistorik Man.
- Triggering when DIV-APU next step doesn't clock length timer, length now enabled, and length set to 64 (256 for CH3) because previously zero → set to 63 instead (255 for CH3).
- Triggering when DIV-APU next step will clock volume envelope: envelope timer reloaded with one greater than normal.
- Noise channel clock shift of 14 or 15 results in LFSR receiving no clocks.
- Clearing sweep direction bit in NR10 after at least one subtraction-mode sweep calculation since last trigger → CH1 immediately disabled. Prevents lowering then raising frequency without re-trigger.

metadata.heading_path: Audio Details > Obscure Behavior > Timing quirks
keywords: envelope sweep timer period 0 treated as 8

text:
## Obscure Audio Behavior (continued)

**Wave RAM corruption on DMG**: Triggering CH3 while it reads a sample byte alters the first four bytes of wave RAM. If reading one of first 4 bytes: only first byte rewritten with read byte. If reading bytes 4-15: first FOUR bytes rewritten with aligned source bytes (e.g., reading byte 9 → bytes 8-11 copied to first 4). Avoid by writing 0 then $80 to NR30 before retriggering. Duck Tales encounters this issue.

**Zombie mode**: Volume can be manually altered mid-playback via NRx2 writes. CGB-02/CGB-04 most consistent:
- If old envelope period was zero and envelope still auto-updating: volume += 1; if decrease mode: volume += 2.
- Mode change (increase↔decrease): volume = 16 - volume.
- Only low 4 bits kept. DMG units have inconsistent behavior.
- Useful consistent pattern: write $V8 to NRx2 for initial volume V, then $08 to increment by 1 during playback (repeat 15 times to decrement by 1).

**HPF implementation**: When all DACs off, master volume disconnected, output = 0. Any DAC on connects HPF capacitor that removes DC component. Charge factor: 0.999958^(4194304/rate). DMG uses 0.999958; MGB&CGB use 0.998943.

text:
# CPU Instruction Set

The Game Boy's SM83 processor has a CISC, variable-length instruction set. The first byte of each instruction is the opcode. Instructions performing identical operations with different parameters can be grouped together.

Placeholders and their values:
| Placeholder | Encoding |
|---|---|
| r8 | 0:b, 1:c, 2:d, 3:e, 4:h, 5:l, 6:[hl], 7:a |
| r16 | 0:bc, 1:de, 2:hl, 3:sp |
| r16stk | 0:bc, 1:de, 2:hl, 3:af |
| r16mem | 0:bc, 1:de, 2:hl+, 3:hl- |
| cond | 0:nz, 1:z, 2:nc, 3:c |
| b3 | 0-7: A 3-bit bit index |
| tgt3 | 0-7: rst's target address / 8 |
| imm8 | The following byte (instruction +1 extra byte) |
| imm16 | Following two bytes, little-endian (instruction +2 extra bytes) |

Note: [hl+] and [hl-] can also be notated [hli] and [hld].

Instruction families are grouped into four blocks by the first two bits of the opcode.

keywords: r8 b c d e h l [hl] a 8-bit register
r16 bc de hl sp 16-bit register
r16stk bc de hl af stack
r16mem bc de hl+ hl- autoincrement
stop two-byte second byte not always ignored

text: ## Block 1: 8-bit register-to-register loads ```text ld r8, r8 7:"0" 6:"1" 5-3:"Dest (r8)" 2-0:"Source (r8)" ``` **Exception**: trying to encode `ld [hl], [hl]` instead yields the `halt` instruction: ```text halt 7:"0" 6:"1" 5:"1" 4:"1" 3:"0" 2:"1" 1:"1" 0:"0" ``` ## Block 2: 8-bit arithmetic ```text add a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"0" 4:"0" 3:"0" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" adc a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"0" 4:"0" 3:"1" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" sub a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"0" 4:"1" 3:"0" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" sbc a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"0" 4:"1" 3:"1" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" and a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"1" 4:"0" 3:"0" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" xor a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"1" 4:"0" 3:"1" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" or a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"1" 4:"1" 3:"0" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" cp a, r8 7:"1" 6:"0" 5:"1" 4:"1" 3:"1" 2-0:"Operand (r8)" ``` ## Block 3 Block 3 includes immediate arithmetic (`add a, imm8`, `adc a, imm8`, `sub a, imm8`, `sbc a, imm8`, `and a, imm8`, `xor a, imm8`, `or a, imm8`, `cp a, imm8`), control flow (`ret cond`, `ret`, `reti`, `jp cond, imm16`, `jp imm16`, `jp hl`, `call cond, imm16`, `call imm16`, `rst tgt3`), stack operations (`pop r16stk`, `push r16stk`), the prefix instruction, I/O loads (`ldh [c], a`, `ldh [imm8], a`, `ld [imm16], a`, `ldh a, [c]`, `ldh a, [imm8]`, `ld a, [imm16]`), SP operations (`add sp, imm8`, `ld hl, sp + imm8`, `ld sp, hl`), and `di`/`ei`. The following opcodes are **invalid**, and hard-lock the CPU until the console is powered off: $D3, $DB, $DD, $E3, $E4, $EB, $EC, $ED, $F4, $FC, and $FD.

text:
# CPU Registers and Flags

## Registers
| 16-bit | Hi | Lo | Name/Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| AF | A | - | Accumulator & Flags |
| BC | B | C | BC |
| DE | D | E | DE |
| HL | H | L | HL |
| SP | - | - | Stack Pointer |
| PC | - | - | Program Counter/Pointer |

Most registers can be accessed as one 16-bit register or two separate 8-bit registers.

## The Flags Register (lower 8 bits of AF)
| Bit | Name | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 7 | Z | Zero flag — set iff result is zero; used by conditional jumps |
| 6 | N | Subtraction flag (BCD) — indicates previous instruction was subtraction |
| 5 | H | Half Carry flag (BCD) — carry for lower 4 bits of result |
| 4 | C | Carry flag — set on overflow/underflow; used by conditional jumps, ADC, SBC, RL, RLA etc. |

Bits 3-0 are unused.

## The Carry Flag Details
Set when:
- 8-bit addition result > $FF
- 16-bit addition result > $FFFF
- Subtraction/comparison result < 0 (like Z80/x86, unlike 65XX/ARM)
- Rotate/shift operation shifts out a "1" bit

metadata.heading_path: CPU Registers and Flags > Registers / Flags Register

SP stack pointer PC program counter
flags Z bit 7 zero N bit 6 subtraction H bit 5 half carry C bit 4 carry
carry flag 8-bit overflow $FF 16-bit overflow $FFFF subtraction negative rotate shift out 1
Z80 x86 carry like 65XX ARM unlike

text:
## The BCD Flags (N, H)

Used by DAA instruction only. N indicates whether the previous instruction was a subtraction; H indicates carry for lower 4 bits of result.
DAA also uses C flag, which must indicate carry for upper 4 bits.
After adding/subtracting two BCD numbers, DAA converts the result to BCD format.
BCD numbers range from $00-$99 rather than $00-$FF.

Because only two flags (C and H) exist for carry-outs of BCD digits:
- DAA is ineffective for 16-bit operations (4 digits)
- Use with INC/DEC has limits (they don't affect C-flag)

text:
# CPU Comparison with Z80

## Comparison with 8080

The Game Boy CPU has more in common with Intel 8080 than Zilog Z80. Missing some 8080 instructions but supports JR and almost all CB-prefixed instructions. All known assemblers use Z80-style syntax.

Unlike 8080/Z80: no dedicated I/O bus, no IN/OUT opcodes. I/O ports accessed via normal LD or new LD ($FF00+n) opcodes.

Removed from 8080: sign flag, parity/overflow flag, and all 12 RET/CALL/JP instructions conditioned on them. Also removed: EX (SP),HL (XTHL) and EX DE,HL (XCHG).

## Comparison with Z80

In addition to removed 8080 instructions:
- All exchange instructions removed (including total absence of second register set)
- All DD- and FD-prefixed instructions missing (no IX/IY registers)
- All ED-prefixed instructions missing (16-bit memory accesses mostly gone, 16-bit arithmetic heavily cut down)
- IN/OUT (C) replaced with LD ($FF00+C)
- Block instructions gone; autoincrementing HL accesses added
- Speed: ~4 MHz Z80 equivalent (8 MHz in CGB double speed); all instruction times rounded to multiple of 4 cycles

ED prefix missing 16-bit arithmetic cut down block instructions gone

text:
# Timer Obscure Behaviour

DIV is the visible part of the system counter, which constantly increments every M-cycle unless CPU is in STOP mode.

## Relation between Timer and Divider Register

The bits of the system counter connect to a multiplexer then to a falling-edge detector. This causes odd behaviors:

- Writing to DIV (resetting system counter) can reset the bit currently selected by TAC's multiplexer, sending an early "Timer tick" and/or "DIV-APU event" pulse.
- Changing TAC's Clock select from a set bit to an unset bit sends a "Timer tick" pulse. Example: if system counter = $3FF0 and TAC = $FC, writing $05 or $06 triggers a tick; $04 or $07 won't.
- On monochrome consoles (DMG), disabling the timer when the selected bit is set sends one "Timer tick". Does NOT happen on CGB.
- On CGB, a TAC write that meets the above conditions AND turns the timer on may or may not send a tick; behavior varies between individual consoles.

text:
## Timer Overflow Behavior

When TIMA overflows, TMA's value is copied to TIMA and the timer flag is set in IF — but one M-cycle later. TIMA equals $00 for the M-cycle after overflow.
Only happens from incrementing; cannot be triggered by manually writing to TIMA.

Unexpected behaviors:
1. Writing to TIMA during cycle A (overflow cycle) acts as if overflow didn't happen! TMA not copied, IF bit 2 not set. Writing to DIV/TAC/other registers won't prevent IF flag or TIMA reload.
2. Writing to TIMA during cycle B will be ignored; TIMA = TMA at end of cycle anyway (TAL cells constantly copy input).
3. Writing to TMA during cycle B copies the new value to TIMA as well, on same cycle.

Technical explanation: Writing to TIMA blocks falling edge detection via AND gate. The "Load" signal stays enabled for cycle B; TAL cells constantly copy input. Write to TIMA goes through but is overwritten by multiplexer emitting TMA's value.

text:
# halt

`halt` pauses the CPU (reducing power consumption). CPU wakes when an interrupt is pending: `[IE] & [IF] != 0`.

With IME set: CPU wakes, interrupt handler called normally before next instruction.

With IME not set:
- No interrupt pending: halt executes normally; resumes on next interrupt (not handled since IME=0)
- Interrupt already pending: halt exits immediately, triggering the **halt bug**

## Halt Bug

When executed with IME=0 and `[IE] & [IF] != 0`, halt ends but PC fails to increment normally.

Under most circumstances: byte after halt is read again (repeats if that byte is another halt).

Special cases:
- **halt preceded by ei**: ei's effect delayed one instruction, so IME still zero. Interrupt serviced and handler called, returns to halt which waits for next interrupt.
- **halt followed by rst**: rst's return address points at the rst itself (not byte after it). A ret would execute the rst again.

If bugged halt is preceded by ei AND followed by rst: the ei case "wins".

text:
# Power-Up Sequence

CPU starts at $0000, not $0100. A boot ROM burned into the CPU is mapped over cartridge ROM initially, responsible for boot-up animation before handing control to game ROM at $0100.

Known boot ROMs:
| Name | Size | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| DMG0 | 256 | Blinks on failed checks, no ® |
| DMG | 256 | Standard monochrome |
| MGB | 256 | One-byte difference to DMG (writes $FF not $01) |
| SGB | 256 | Forwards logo to SGB BIOS, no checks |
| SGB2 | 256 | Same diff to SGB as MGB has to DMG |
| CGB0 | 256+1792 | Does not init wave RAM |
| CGB | 256+1792 | Split in two parts, header in middle |
| AGB0 | 256+1792 | Increments B register for GBA identification |
| AGB | 256+1792 | Fixes "logo TOCTTOU" |

## Monochrome Models (DMG0, DMG, MGB)
Boot ROM reads logo from header, unpacks to VRAM, scrolls it down. No cartridge = black box ($FF reads). After scrolling: plays "ba-ding!", reads logo again comparing to stored copy, checks header checksum. If either fails → boot ROM locks up.

Finally writes to BANK register at $FF50 (unmaps boot ROM). `ldh [$FF50], a` at $00FE is two bytes; first cartridge instruction executes at $0100. A register value passed: DMG writes $01, MGB writes $FF.

DMG0 variant: performs checks BEFORE display; on failure screen blinks white/black alternately. Lacks ® symbol.

text:
## Super Game Boy (SGB, SGB2)
Boot ROMs do NOT perform header checks. Set up logo in VRAM from header, then send entire header to SGB BIOS via packet transfer using bytes $F1, $F3, $F5, $F7, $F9, $FB. Unmaps itself without checks; SGB BIOS on SNES verifies logo and checksum instead.

Packet-sending routine: transferring a set bit takes one cycle less than reset bit; total time depends on cartridge header contents. Waits 4 VBlanks after each packet.

## Color Models (CGB0, CGB, AGB0, AGB)
Boot ROM is 2048 bytes, split in two parts: $0000-00FF and $0200-08FF (to access cartridge header at $0100-014F).

Unpacks logo to VRAM + copies to HRAM buffer. Reads/decompresses again for smaller "GAME BOY" logo below big one. Sets up compatibility palettes, plays animation.

During animation: if CGB compatibility byte bit 7 reset (monochrome game), user can pick palette override; each choice delays end by 30 frames.

Checks logo from HRAM buffer (only first half checked despite full copy). Fades BG palettes to white, sets hardware to compatibility mode. Writes $11 to unmap.

CGB0: does NOT init wave RAM ($FF30-$FF3F); causes different title screen music in R-Type.

text:
## Compatibility Palettes
Boot ROM auto-colorizes monochrome games on GBC. In DMG compatibility mode, CGB palettes still used: BG uses palette 0 (attribute map all zeros), OBJ uses OBP0/OBP1 depending on bit 4 of attributes. BGP/OBP0/OBP1 index into CGB palettes instead of DMG shades.

Palette ID algorithm:
1. Check old licensee code ($33 → check new licensee = "01"; otherwise check old = $01). Failure → palette ID $00.
2. Sum all 16 game title bytes as "title checksum"
3. Find checksum in table, record index. Not found → $00. Index ≤ 64 → use as-is. Otherwise correct by fourth letter of title (search another table; row × 14 added to index).

Resulting ID picks 3 palettes from a complex mechanism. User can override with button combos during animation.

If ID is $43 or $58: Nintendo logo tilemap written to VRAM (for games animating the logo).

## Logo Check and Bypass
Logo check deters piracy via trademark law (*Sega v. Accolade* ruled it's okay if necessary for console operation).

Bypass methods:
- TOCTTOU: exploit timing between logo read for display vs verification (patched on later AGB; requires custom hardware)
- CGB/AGB only: boot ROM copies full logo to HRAM but checks only first half. Top-half-correct/bottom-half-wrong logo passes.

text:
## Console State After Boot ROM Hand-off
WRAM and HRAM are random on power-up. External cartridge RAM usually contains random garbage; check for known sequence before accessing saves.
Emulator uninitialized RAM behavior is inconsistent — enable "break on uninitialized RAM read" exception.

### CPU Registers After Boot
| Register | DMG0 | DMG | MGB | SGB | SGB2 |

| A | $01 | $01 | $FF | $01 | $FF |
| F | Z=0 N=0 H=0 C=0 | Z=1 N=0 H=? C=? | Z=1 N=0 H=? C=? | Z=0 N=0 H=0 C=0 | Z=0 N=0 H=0 C=0 |
| B | $FF | $00 | $00 | $00 | $00 |
| C | $13 | $13 | $13 | $14 | $14 |
| E | $C1 | $D8 | $D8 | $00 | $00 |
| H | $84 | $01 | $01 | $C0 | $C0 |
| L | $03 | $4D | $4D | $60 | $60 |

| SP | $FFFE | $FFFE | $FFFE | $FFFE | $FFFE |

### CGB/AGB Registers After Boot

| PC | $0100 | $0100 | $0100 | $0100 |

text:
# CGB Registers

This chapter covers Game Boy Color registers not in normal categories. Most CGB registers are in the Video Display chapter.

## Unlocking CGB Functions
Byte $0143 in cartridge header must be changed: $80 for dual CGB/monochrome support, $C0 for CGB-only. Otherwise CGB operates in monochrome "Non CGB" compatibility mode.

## Detecting CGB (and GBA)
A register = $11 after startup indicates CGB or GBA. When A=$11, examine B-register bit 0: cleared = CGB, set = GBA (for repaired color palette data matching).

## LCD VRAM DMA Transfers (HDMA)
### $FF51-$FF52 — HDMA1/HDMA2: Source address [write-only]
Specifies transfer source. Should be ROM/SRAM/WRAM ($0000-7FF0 or $A000-DFF0). Lower 4 bits ignored (treated as 0). VRAM source causes garbage.

### $FF53-$FF54 — HDMA3/HDMA4: Destination address [write-only]
Specifies destination in $8000-9FF0. Only bits 12-4 respected; lower 4 and upper 3 ignored (always VRAM).

### $FF55 — HDMA5: Length/mode/start
Writing starts transfer. Lower 7 bits = length ($10-$800 bytes, values $00-$7F). Bit 7 = mode:
- **Bit 7=0 (General-Purpose DMA)**: All data at once; program halted until complete. Blindly copies even if LCD accessing VRAM — use only when display off, during VBlank, or short HBlank blocks. After completion FF55 = $FF.
- **Bit 7=1 (HBlank DMA)**: Transfers $10 bytes per HBlank (LY=0-143). No transfer during VBlank (LY=144-153), resumes at LY=00. CPU halted during transfers but runs between blocks. Don't change destination VRAM bank or source ROM/RAM bank until complete.

Reading FF55 returns remaining length ($FF = complete). Terminate HBlank by writing 0 to bit 7; lower 7 bits show remaining blocks (minus 1), bit 7 reads as 1. Stopping doesn't set HDMA1-4 to $FF.

**Warning**: Don't start HBlank DMA during HBlank period (STAT mode 0). Destination overflow stops transfer prematurely.

text:
## HDMA Transfer Timings
Both Normal and Double Speed: ~8 μs per $10 byte block. 8 M-cycles normal speed, 16 fast M-cycles double speed. Always 2 bytes/μs transferred.
Older MBC controllers (MBC1-3) and slower ROMs not guaranteed to support General-Purpose or HBlank DMA.
VBlank allows ~2280 bytes transfer = up to 142.5 tiles.

## VRAM Banks
CGB has twice DMG's VRAM, banked with different purposes per bank.

### $FF4F — VBK: VRAM Bank
Only bit 0 matters; all other bits ignored. Reading returns current bank in bit 0, other bits set to 1.

**VRAM Bank 1**: Split like bank 0. $8000-97FF stores tiles (accessible same time as bank 0). $9800-9FFF contains attributes for corresponding Tile Maps.

## $FF4D — KEY1/SPD: Prepare Speed Switch
Bit layout:
- Bit 7 (read-only): Current speed. 0 = Normal, 1 = Double-speed.
- Bit 0 (R/W): Switch armed. 0 = No, 1 = Armed.

Speed switch procedure:
```
IF KEY1_BIT7 != DESIRED_SPEED THEN
   IE = $00; JOYP = $30; KEY1 = $01; STOP
ENDIF
```
CGB starts in Normal Speed. Double speed increases power consumption.

In Double Speed Mode, twice as fast: CPU (2.10 MHz, 1 M-cycle ≈ 0.5 μs), Timer/Divider Registers, Serial Port, OAM DMA.
Unchanged: LCD Video Controller, HDMA Transfer to VRAM, all Sound Timings/Frequencies.

After `stop`: CPU stops for 2050 M-cycles (8200 T-cycles). DIV doesn't tick; some audio events not processed. VRAM/OAM locking frozen — behavior depends on PPU mode at start: Mode 0/1 = no video memory access, black pixels; Mode 2 = VRAM accessible but not OAM (BG renders, sprites don't); Mode 3 = everything works correctly.

text:
## $FF56 — RP: Infrared Communications Port (CGB only)
Input/output data through CGB's built-in IR port. For reading, bits 6-7 must be set ($C0+); bit 0 cleared to avoid receiving own signal. Reset to $00 after use to reduce power.

Bit layout:
- Bits 7-6: Read enable. 0 = Disable (bit 1 reads 1), 3 = Enable.
- Bit 1 (read-only): Receiving. 0 = IR signal received, 1 = Normal.
- Bit 0: Emitting. 0 = LED off, 1 = LED on.

Receiver adapts to ambient IR level; permanent ON treated as normal after a while. GBA does not include IR port.

## $FF4C — KEY0/SYS: CPU Mode Select (CGB only)
Written only by CGB boot ROM; locked after BANK register write unmapps boot ROM. Cannot be changed without reset.
- Bit 2: DMG compatibility mode. 0 = Disabled (full CGB), 1 = Enabled (DMG-only cartridges).

**PGB Mode**: Setting bit 3 may relate to external LCD control. Not well researched.

## $FF6C — OPRI: Object Priority Mode (CGB only)
Flag for object priority mode. DMG prioritizes by x-coordinate; CGB by OAM location. Set by boot ROM after checking CGB compatibility.
- Bit 0: Priority mode. 0 = CGB-style, 1 = DMG-style.

Effect if PGB value written to KEY0 before STOP executed (instant effect). No instant effect in CGB/DMG mode after boot ROM unmapped.

## $FF70 — SVBK/WBK: WRAM Bank (CGB only)
CGB has 32 KiB internal RAM in 8 banks of 4 KiB. Bank 0 always at $C000-CFFF; banks 1-7 selectable into $D000-DFFF.
- Bits 2-0: WRAM bank. Writing value maps corresponding bank to $D000-DFFF, except 0 which maps bank 1.

## Undocumented Registers
$FF72-$FF73: Fully R/W, initial $00 (CGB only).
$FF74: Fully R/W in CGB mode ($00); read-only locked at $FF otherwise.
$FF75: Only bits 4-6 R/W, initial 0 (CGB only).

text:
# MBCs (Memory Bank Controllers)

The Game Boy's 16-bit address bus offers limited space for ROM and RAM. MBC chips in the cartridge expand available address space via bank switching.

Required/preferred MBC type specified at byte $0147 of ROM (cartridge header).

## MBC Timing Issues
Only MBC5 is guaranteed by Nintendo to support CGB Double Speed Mode's tighter timing. Older MBCs (MBC1-3) may not be fast enough, though self-made MBC1-EPROM cards appear stable in Double Speed. Possible workaround: use Double Speed only during periods using internal RAM code/data.

## MBC Unmapped RAM Bank Access
If an unmapped RAM bank is selected (out of bounds), most MBCs wrap around to a valid address:
`((address - external_ram_start) + (active_ram_bank * ram_bank_size)) % max_external_ram_size`

text:
# No MBC (ROM Only)

Games ≤ 32 KiB ROM don't require an MBC chip. ROM directly mapped at $0000-7FFF.
Optionally up to 8 KiB RAM connectable at $A000-BFFF using a discrete logic decoder instead of full MBC chip.

keywords: 32 KiB ROM only no MBC $0000-7FFF direct map optional 8 KiB RAM $A000-BFFF discrete decoder

text:
# MBC1
Max 2 MiB ROM and/or 32 KiB RAM. First MBC chip; newer chips work similarly.

Default: up to 512 KiB ROM with 32 KiB banked RAM. Some carts wire the 2-bit RAM banking register as ROM banking extension for up to 2 MiB ROM (fixed 8 KiB RAM). All MBC1 carts ≥ 1 MiB use this alternate wiring.

Memory range $0000-7FFF used for both reading ROM and writing MBC control registers.

## Memory Regions
**$0000-3FFF**: First 16 KiB (bank 00). In 1 MiB+ carts, mode 1 allows second banking register to apply here too (banks $20/$40/$60 for large ROM, or $10/$20/$30 for MBC1M).

**$4000-7FFF**: Any of further 16 KiB banks. If main 5-bit register = 0, reads as bank 1. For 1 MiB+ carts: banks accessible via $0000-3FFF region not accessible here; maps to +1 bank ($21/$41/$61 or $11/$21/$31).

**$A000-BFFF**: External RAM if any. Only accessible when RAM enabled; otherwise reads return open bus (often $FF, not guaranteed), writes ignored. 8 KiB or 32 KiB (four 8K banks). 32 KiB only in carts ≤ 512 KiB ROM.

## Registers (all default to $00 on power-up; ROM bank treated as $01)
**$0000-1FFF — RAM Enable [write-only]**: Write value with lower nibble = $A to enable. Any other value disables. Recommend disabling after access to protect contents.

**$2000-3FFF — ROM Bank Number [write-only]**: 5-bit register ($01-$1F) for $4000-7FFF region. Higher bits discarded. $00 → $01 (can't duplicate bank 0). Over-range values masked to cart size. For ≤256 KiB carts, setting bit 5 prevents 00→01 translation while selecting bank 0.

**$4000-5FFF — RAM Bank / Upper ROM Bits [write-only]**: 2-bit register for RAM bank ($00-$03, 32 KiB carts) or upper bits (bits 5-6 of ROM number, 1 MiB+ carts). If neither large enough, does nothing.

**$6000-7FFF — Banking Mode Select [write-only]**: Bit 0 selects mode. 0 = simple (default): $0000-3FFF and $A000-BFFF locked to bank 0. 1 = advanced: both regions can be bank-switched via $4000-5FFF register.

Formula for large carts: `Selected ROM Bank = (Secondary Bank << 5) + ROM Bank`

text:
## MBC1M: 1 MiB Multi-Game Compilation Carts
Alternative wiring ignores top bit of main ROM banking register (effectively 4-bit, though full 5-bit still used for 00→01 translation). 2-bit register applied to bits 4-5 of bank number (not usual bits 5-6).
In mode 1: 2-bit register selects banks $00, $10, $20, or $30 (vs. usual $00, $20, $40, $60). Uses mode 1 remapping of $0000-3FFF to switch games; each game thinks it's running from a 256 KiB cart.

Identification: Nintendo copyright header in bank $10. Bad dumps have duplicate content in banks $10-$1F (dupe of $00-$0F) and $30-$3F (dupe of $20-$2F). Known bad dump: Mortal Kombat I & II.

Conversion to regular MBC1: Increase ROM size to 2 MiB, duplicate each sub-ROM ($00-$0F → $10-$1F, original $10-$1F → $20-$2F duplicated into $30-$3F).

### Addressing (MBC1M)
**$0000-3FFF**: Mode 0 = bank 0. Mode 1 = from $4000-5FFF register at bits 18-19.
**$4000-7FFF**: Both modes = $4000-5FFF at bits 18-19, $2000-3FFF (bit 4 unused) at bits 16-13.

text:
# MBC2
Max 256 KiB ROM and 512×4 bits RAM.

## Memory Regions
**$0000-3FFF**: First 16 KiB of ROM (bank 0).

**$4000-7FFF**: ROM banks $01-$0F. Same as MBC1 but only 16 total banks.

**$A000-A1FF**: Built-in RAM — 512 half-bytes (built into MBC2 chip). Requires external battery for save. Only lower 4 bits used; upper 4 bits undefined, don't rely on them.

**$A200-BFFF**: 15 echoes of $A000-A1FF. Only bottom 9 address bits index internal RAM (access repeats). Same 4-bit limitation as $A000-A1FF.

## Registers
### $0000-3FFF — RAM Enable + ROM Bank Number [write-only]
Single address range controls both RAM enable and ROM bank. Bit 8 of address determines which:

**Bit 8 clear (even upper byte)**: Controls RAM enable. Lower 4 bits = $A → enabled; any other value → disabled. Addresses: $0000-00FF, $0200-02FF, ..., $3E00-3EFF. Default: disabled.

**Bit 8 set (odd upper byte)**: Controls ROM bank at $4000-7FFF. Lower 4 bits = bank number. Bank 0 → bank 1. Addresses: $0100-01FF, $0300-03FF, ..., $3F00-3FFF. Default: bank 1.

RAM enable ROM bank combined $0000-3FFF bit 8 of address determines function

text:
# MBC3
Max 2 MiB ROM and/or 32 KiB RAM plus built-in RTC. RTC requires external 32.768 kHz quartz oscillator and battery.

## Memory Regions
**$0000-3FFF**: First 16 KiB (bank 00).
**$4000-7FFF**: Banks $01-$7F. Same as MBC1 except banks $20, $40, $60 are now accessible.
**$A000-BFFF**: External RAM bank or RTC register depending on selection.

## Registers
**$0000-1FFF — RAM and Timer Enable [write-only]**: Same as MBC1. $0A enables RAM AND RTC registers; $00 disables both.

**$2000-3FFF — ROM Bank Number [write-only]**: Full 7 bits written directly ($01-$7F). $00 → bank $01 (same as MBC1).

**$4000-5FFF — RAM Bank / RTC Register Select [write-only]**:
| Value | Selection |
|---|---|
| $00-$07 | Corresponding RAM bank |
| $08-$0C | Corresponding RTC register |

**$6000-7FFF — Latch Clock Data [write-only]**: Write $00 then $01 to latch current time into RTC registers. Latched data won't change until latched again (allows reading while clock ticks).

## RTC Registers
| Register | Name | Range |
|---|---|---|
| $08 | Seconds | 0-59 ($00-$3B) |
| $09 | Minutes | 0-59 ($00-$3B) |
| $0A | Hours | 0-23 ($00-$17) |
| $0B | Day Counter (lower 8 bits) | $00-$FF |
| $0C | Day Counter upper bit + flags | Bit 0: Day counter MSB; Bit 6: Halt (0=Active, 1=Stop); Bit 7: Carry overflow |

Day counter: 9 bits total, range 0-511 ($000-$1FF). Carry bit set on overflow; remains until reset. Store offset in battery RAM to count beyond 511 days (add counter to offset each read, reset counter).

**Delays**: Wait 4 μs (4 M-cycles normal speed) between separate RTC register accesses.

text:
# MBC5
Maps up to 8 MiB (64 Mbit) ROM. First MBC guaranteed for CGB Double Speed mode.

## Memory Regions
**$0000-3FFF**: First 16 KiB (bank 00).
**$4000-7FFF**: Banks $00-$1FF. Unlike other MBCs, bank 0 is actually bank 0 (not remapped to 1). Up to 512 banks.
**$A000-BFFF**: RAM banks $00-$0F if any. Sizes: 8 KiB, 32 KiB, or 128 KiB.

## Registers
**$0000-1FFF — RAM Enable [write-only]**: Same as MBC1. $0A enables; $00 disables. Actual MBCs enable when bottom 4 bits = $A ($0A, $1A, etc.), but relying on this is not recommended.

**$2000-2FFF — ROM Bank Lower Bits [write-only]**: 8 least significant bits of bank number. Writing 0 gives actual bank 0 (unlike other MBCs).

**$3000-3FFF — ROM Bank Upper Bit [write-only]**: 9th bit of bank number.

**$4000-5FFF — RAM Bank Number [write-only]**: $00-$0F maps corresponding external RAM bank to $A000-BFFF.

## Rumble
On cartridges with rumble motor, bit 3 of RAM bank register connects to rumble circuitry instead of RAM chip. Setting bit 3 = 1 enables rumble; reset to disable. Control intensity by turning on/off repeatedly (see Pokémon Pinball examples).

text:
# The Cartridge Header
Header at $0100-$014F provides game info and expected hardware.

## $0100-0103 — Entry Point
Boot ROM jumps to $0100 after displaying logo. Most games: `nop` + `jp $0150` (4 bytes).

## $0104-0133 — Nintendo Logo
48-byte bitmap displayed on power-on. Must match exact dump or boot ROM locks up:
```
CE ED 66 66 CC 0D 00 0B 03 73 00 83 00 0C 00 0D
00 08 11 1F 88 89 00 0E DC CC 6E E6 DD DD D9 99
BB BB 67 63 6E 0E EC CC DD DC 99 9F BB B9 33 3E
```
Bytes $0104-$011B = top half, $011C-$0133 = bottom half. Each nibble encodes 4 pixels (MSB=leftmost). Groups laid out top-to-bottom, left-to-right. Monochrome models upscale by factor of 2.
CGB+ only checks top half ($18 bytes).

## $0134-0143 — Title
Upper-case ASCII game title. If < 16 chars, pad with $00. On later carts, parts overlap with other fields (reducing actual title to 15 or 11 chars).

## $013F-0142 — Manufacturer Code
Newer cartridges: 4-character manufacturer code (uppercase ASCII). Purpose unknown.

## $0143 — CGB Flag
Determines Color mode vs monochrome compatibility:
| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $80 | Supports CGB enhancements, backwards compatible with DMG |
| $C0 | CGB only (bit 6 ignored by hardware; same as $80) |
Setting bit 7 triggers write to KEY0 register for CPU mode selection.

text:
## $0144-0145 — New Licensee Code
Two-character ASCII publisher code. Only meaningful if old licensee ($014B) = $33 (post-SGB games).
Notable codes: 01=Nintendo R&D1, 08=Capcom, 13=EA, 18/38=Hudson Soft, 31=Nintendo, 32=Bandai, 34/54=Konami, 78=THQ.

## $0146 — SGB Flag
$03 = game supports SGB functions. Any other value (typically $00) causes SGB to ignore command packets.

## $0147 — Cartridge Type
Indicates mapper and hardware:
| Code | Type |
|---|---|
| $00 | ROM ONLY |
| $01-$03 | MBC1 / +RAM / +RAM+BATTERY |
| $05-$06 | MBC2 / +BATTERY |
| $0F-$13 | MBC3 variants (TIMER, RAM, BATTERY) |
| $19-$1E | MBC5 variants (RAM, RUMBLE, BATTERY) |
| $20 | MBC6 |
| $22 | MBC7+SENSOR+RUMBLE+RAM+BATTERY |
| $FC-$FF | POCKET CAMERA, BANDAI TAMA5, HuC3, HuC1 |

## $0148 — ROM Size
`32 KiB × (1 << value)`: $00=32KiB(2 banks), $01=64KiB(4), ..., $08=8MiB(512). Unofficial sizes: $52=1.1MiB, $53=1.2MiB, $54=1.5MiB (no known cartridges use these).

## $0149 — RAM Size
$00=None, $02=8KiB(1 bank), $03=32KiB(4 banks), $04=128KiB(16 banks), $05=64KiB(8 banks). MBC2's built-in 512×4 bits not counted here.

## $014A — Destination Code
$00 = Japan (and possibly overseas); $01 = Overseas only.

text:
## $014B — Old Licensee Code
Pre-SGB publisher byte. Value $33 means use new licensee codes ($0144-0145) instead.
Notable old codes: 01=Nintendo, 08=Capcom, 13=EA, 18=Hudson Soft, 31=Nintendo, 32=Bandai, 34=Konami, 78=THQ. Full table has ~150 known codes.

## $014C — Mask ROM Version Number
Usually $00.

## $014D — Header Checksum
8-bit checksum of bytes $0134-$014C:
```c
uint8_t checksum = 0;
for (address = 0x0134; address <= 0x014C; address++)
    checksum = checksum - rom[address] - 1;
```
Boot ROM verifies this. Mismatch → boot ROM locks up, cartridge won't run.

## $014E-014F — Global Checksum
16-bit big-endian sum of all ROM bytes (excluding these two checksum bytes). Not verified by boot ROM; only checked by Pokémon Stadium's "GB Tower" emulator.

text:
## Game Genie (ROM Patches)

Game Shark and Game Genie are external cartridge adapters plugged between the Game Boy and the actual game cartridge.

Game Genie codes consist of nine-digit hex numbers, formatted as `ABC-DEF-GHI`. Digit meaning:
- `AB`: New data to write
- `FCDE`: Memory address, XORed by $F000
- `GI`: Old data, XORed by $BA and rotated left by two
- `H`: Unknown — possibly checksum and/or other purpose

The address should be in ROM area $0000-$7FFF. The adapter permanently compares address/old data with the address/data being read by the game, replacing data with new data if necessary. This method (more or less) prohibits unwanted patching of wrong memory banks. It may also be possible to patch external RAM. Newer devices reportedly allow specifying only the first six digits optionally.

Three codes can be used at once.

keywords: Game Genie nine-digit hex ABC-DEF-GHI format external cartridge adapter
AB new data FCDE address XOR $F000 GI old data XOR $BA rotate left 2 H unknown checksum
ROM area $0000-$7FFF compare address old data replace new data prevent wrong bank patching
external RAM patch possible newer devices first six digits optional

text:
## Game Shark (RAM Patches)

Game Shark codes consist of eight hexadecimal digits:
| Bits | Field |
|---|---|
| 0-1 | SRAM bank |
| 2-3 | New value |
| 4-7 | Address |

Example: cheat code `010238CD` switches to SRAM bank $01, and writes $02 at address $CD38.

Patching is implemented by hooking the original VBlank interrupt handler, re-writing RAM values each frame. This steals some CPU time and cannot be used to patch program code in ROM (only RAM). 10-25 codes can be used simultaneously.

text:
# MBC6

MBC6 is an unusual MBC containing two separately switchable ROM banks ($4000 and $6000) and RAM banks ($A000 and $B000), SRAM, and an 8 Mbit Macronix MX29F008TC-14 flash memory chip. Only used in one game: Net de Get: Minigame @ 100, which uses the Mobile Adapter to connect to the web to download mini-games onto local flash.

Both ROM banks and both RAM banks are views into the same ROM and RAM but with separately adjustable offsets. Since banked regions are smaller, effective number of banks is twice normal: 8 KiB ROM banks instead of 16 KiB, 4 KiB RAM banks instead of 8 KiB.

## Memory Regions
| Range | Description |
|---|---|
| $0000-3FFF | ROM Bank 00 (Read Only) — first 16 KiB of ROM |
| $4000-5FFF | ROM/Flash Bank A, banks 00-7F (R/W for flash, RO for ROM), switchable independently of Bank B |
| $6000-7FFF | ROM/Flash Bank B, banks 00-7F (R/W for flash, RO for ROM), switchable independently of Bank A |
| $A000-AFFF | RAM Bank A, banks 00-07 (Read/Write), switchable independently of Bank B |
| $B000-BFFF | RAM Bank B, banks 00-07 (Read/Write), switchable independently of Bank A |

keywords: MBC6 two separately switchable ROM banks $4000 $6000 RAM banks $A000 $B000 SRAM flash MX29F008TC-14 8 Mbit
Net de Get Minigame @ 100 Mobile Adapter web download mini-games local flash
8 KiB ROM banks not 16 KiB 4 KiB RAM banks not 8 KiB twice effective banks
$0000-3FFF bank 00 first 16 KiB $4000-5FFF Bank A $6000-7FFF Bank B independent
$A000-AFFF RAM Bank A $B000-BFFF RAM Bank B 8 banks each independently switchable

text:
## MBC6 Registers
| Range | Register | Description |
|---|---|---|
| $0000-03FF | RAM Enable (WO) | Same as MBC1: $0A enables R/W external RAM, $00 disables |
| $0400-07FF | RAM Bank A Number (WO) | Selects active RAM Bank A ($A000-AFFF), banks 00-07 |
| $0800-0BFF | RAM Bank B Number (WO) | Selects active RAM Bank B ($B000-BFFF), banks 00-07 |
| $0C00-0FFF | Flash Enable (WO) | Lowest bit only: 0 = disable (/CE stays disabled), 1 = enable flash chip access |
| $1000 | Flash Write Enable (WO) | Lowest bit only: maps to /WP pin on flash. 0 = default after power-up, protects sector 0 and hidden region from erase/program. Sectors 1-7 unaffected; always erasable/programmable if Flash Enable is on |
| $2000-27FF | ROM/Flash Bank A Number (WO) | Active bank number for ROM/Flash Bank A |
| $2800-2FFF | ROM/Flash Bank A Select (WO) | 00 = select ROM, 08 = select flash mapped into Bank A |
| $3000-37FF | ROM/Flash Bank B Number (WO) | Active bank number for ROM/Flash Bank B |
| $3800-3FFF | ROM/Flash Bank B Select (WO) | 00 = select ROM, 08 = select flash mapped into Bank B |

text:
## Flash Commands

The Macronix MX29F008TC-14 flash chip is mapped directly into Bank A or B address space. Standard flash access commands apply.

Command sequence: write $AA to $Y555, then $55 to $XAAA (where X=4/6 and Y=5/7 depending on bank region), followed by command-specific bytes.

| Sequence | Command |
|---|---|
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $80→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $30→addr | Erase sector (128 KiB region to $FF) |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $80→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $10→$Y555 | Erase chip (entire 1 MiB to $FF) |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $60→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $04→$Y555 | Erase hidden region* (256 bytes to $FF) |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $90→$Y555 | ID mode (JEDEC ID C2,81 at $XXX0,$XXX1) |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $77→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $77→$Y555 | Read hidden region |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $A0→$Y555 | Program mode |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $60→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $E0→$Y555 | Program hidden region* |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $60→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $40→$Y555 | Unprotect sector 0* |
| $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $60→$Y555, $AA→$Y555, $55→$XAAA, $20→$Y555 | Protect sector 0* |
| $F0→any | Exit any command mode |

Commands marked * require Flash Write Enable bit = 1.

## Programming Procedure
Erase commands cause flash to read status bytes instead of values. Status bit 7 ($80) = operation finished → exit with $F0. Bit 4 ($10) = timeout. Bit 1 ($02) = sector 0 protection enabled.

Programming: erase sector first, activate program mode, write 128 aligned bytes, re-write final address (not $F0) to commit, wait for status completion, write $F0 to exit. If sector not erased first, programming may only AND bytes or fail entirely. Only way to set bits back to 1 is erase entire sector.

Erase sector: last byte ($30) written to address within target sector. Eight sectors of 128 KiB each. Bank = sector × 16 (e.g., sector 2 → bank 32 at $2000, write $30 to $4000-$5FFF).

Erase chip erases all 1 MiB but NOT hidden region. If sector 0 protected, only sectors 1-7 erased.

Hidden region: 256 bytes accessible via read hidden region command. Program in two 128-byte chunks using program hidden region command twice.

text:
# MBC7

MBC7 (Memory Bank Controller 7) is an MBC containing a 2-axis accelerometer (ADXL202E) and a 256 byte EEPROM (93LC56). $A000-BFFF does not directly address the EEPROM, as most MBCs do, but rather contains several registers that can be read or written one at a time. This makes EEPROM access very slow due to needing multiple writes per address.

## Memory

### $0000-3FFF — ROM Bank 00 (Read Only)
Contains the first 16 KiB of the ROM.

### $4000-7FFF — ROM Bank 00-7F (Read Only)
Same as for MBC5. (Bank 0 mapping needs confirmation).

keywords: MBC7 Memory Bank Controller 7 2-axis accelerometer ADXL202E 256 byte EEPROM 93LC56
$A000-BFFF registers not direct EEPROM access slow multiple writes per address
$0000-3FFF ROM bank 00 first 16 KiB read only
$4000-7FFF ROM bank 00-7F same as MBC5 bank 0 mapping needs confirmation

text:
## Registers

### $A000-AFFF — RAM Registers (Read/Write)
Must be enabled via $0000 and $4000 region writes, otherwise reads return $FF and writes do nothing. Registers are addressed through bits 4-7 of the address. Bits 0-3 and 8-11 are ignored.

Accelerometer data must be latched before reading. Data is 16-bit and centered at the value $81D0. Earth's gravity affects the value by roughly $70, with larger acceleration providing a larger range. Maximum range is unknown.

keywords: $A000-AFFF RAM registers read/write must enable via $0000 and $4000 region writes
bits 4-7 address register bits 0-3 and 8-11 ignored disabled reads $FF
accelerometer data must be latched before reading 16-bit centered at $81D0
Earth gravity affects value roughly $70 larger acceleration larger range maximum unknown

text:
### Ax0x/Ax1x — Latch Accelerometer (Write Only)

Write $55 to Ax0x to erase the latched data (reset back to $8000), then $AA to Ax1x to latch the accelerometer and update the addressable registers. Reads return $FF. Other writes do not appear to do anything (partially unconfirmed).

You cannot re-latch the accelerometer value without first erasing it; attempts to do so yield no change.

Once the latch for a sample update has been triggered, the CPU should not be put into HALT for at least 1.2 milliseconds since HALTing turns off the PHI cartridge signal (the System Clock) which the MBC7 accelerometer sensor relies on. If that clock is turned off too soon, the X and Y values will have significantly more noise in the sampled data. The 1.2 ms duration is the same regardless of whether or not the CPU is in CGB Double-speed mode.
